text: BAGARIA, a river of Sicily falling into the bay of Palermo, and giving name to a district about 9 m. from Palermo, which is much resorted to as a summer-residence and for quail-shooting.
BAGATYR, a Greek colony in Russia, in the gov. of Ekaterinoslav, in the district of Mariopol, on the l. bank of the Voltschia.
BAGBOROUGH (West), a parish in Somersetshire, 9 m. NW of Taunton. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 449.
BAGBY, a chapelry in the parish of Kirkby- Knowle, N. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SE of Thirsk. Pop. with Islebeck 317.

BAGDAD, a pashalik of Turkey, forming the SE part of the sultan's dominions in Asia. Its general boundaries are the pash. of Diarbekir on the NW; the pash. of Van, with the Kurdish territory of Julamerik on the N and NE; the Persian prov. of Irak- Ajemi and Khuzistan on the E; the Persian gulf and the Nejed, or Arabia Deserta on the S; and the Syrian desert and the Euphrates on the W. Its general outline is that of a triangle, having its apex resting on the head of the Persian gulf; and its base traced in the Karaja-dagh, and mountains of Kurdistan; while the Aiagha-dagh, or chain of Zagros, and the Shat-el-Arab, define the E side, and the Euphrates may be regarded as tracing the W side to near the parallel of 34°, to the S of which, that great river has a considerable strip of territory belonging to this pashalik on its W side. This extensive territory stretches from NW to SE, between the parallels of 37° and 44°, above 800 m.; while its medium breadth may be taken at 200 m. It comprehends the whole of the ancient Babylonia or Chaldaea-which after the Macedonian conquest received the appellation of Mesopotamia, or the country between the two rivers—the Euphrates and the Tigris, namely,—and the country of Assyria Proper. Mesopotamia is the AL-JEZIRAH of the Arabs, and the IRAK-ARABI of the Persians. These names are, however, used in the present day to distinguish that portion of the country which lies to the N of the city of Bagdad, from that on the S; the former is Al-Jezirah or 'the Island;' while the latter is known as Irak-Arabi. Assyria Proper is the Lower Kurdistan of modern geography. I. The Karaja-dagh or Mount Masius, and its continuation SE, form for 170 m. the northern boundary of this extensive region, extending from WNW to ESE, or from the source of the Tigris to the village of Dugher, and thus far separating the plains of Mesopotamia from the valley of the Upper Tigris. The range of Sinjar lies to the SE of Merdin, and occupies a large space between the Khabur and Hualli rivers. It is of a semicircular form, 50 m. in length from NW to SE, and as much from NE to SW. On the NE it is connected with a low range called the Babel hills. It is a lofty range, considerably higher than the Karaja-dagh, rising 2,000 ft. above the plain, in a gradual ascent from the extremities of the range towards the centre. Throughout all this extensive range there is not a single large town, but there are populous villages. Of these, however, little is known, as neither Turks nor Kurds nor Arabs dare venture amongst them, the inhabitants being Yezidis, and the declared enemies of all other sects. Snow remains on this range for a great part of the year. According to Rousseau's description of the pashalik of Bagdad, the range of Sinjar runs across an immense plain between the rivers last mentioned, which, during the months of March and April, resembles a prairie covered with verdure, sprinkled with aromatic flowers, and watered by numerous springs, which their snowy fountains sometimes convert into large and impetuous torrents; and the summit of the range in many places presents a flat and fertile soil. Barley I would seem, ran SE to the Tigris, by way of Sinjar and millet are produced in abundance in the lower grounds; and the raisins and figs which are here grown are renowned throughout the East for their exquisite flavour and taste.—The Jebel-Hamrin is a range of small elevation. It runs N and NE, from the hilly tract on the Euphrates to the Tigris, crosses that stream, and then runs SE as far as Susiana. On the southern side, where it is separated from the Arabian desert, Mesopotamia is bounded by a hilly tract on both sides of the river, which occupied 13 marches of Xenophon, with the army of the younger Cyrus. These hills abound with forests, which supply the city of Bagdad with firewood.—Amongst the plains of Mesopotamia, that of Carræ, SE of Orfa, where the Parthians defeated Crassus, and that of Sinjar, where a degree of the meridian was measured by the Arabian astronomers, are the most celebrated. Of the two great rivers encompassing this country, the Euphrates, from its entrance on the plains at Juliopolis, to Daradax, forms its western boundary on the side of Syria, for more than 150 geog. m.; from thence to the Median wall, where a canal branched off to the Tigris at Sitace, opposite the confluence of the Diallah, this magnificent stream runs a course of more than 350 geog. m. in a direct distance SE; but as it makes a great many bends or elbows, its actual course is double that length. From Bir to Bussora, the course of the Euphrates is estimated by Chesney at 1,143 m.; and from Bir upwards to the source of its eastern branch near Malasgird, about 500 m. more. On the NE and SE the Tigris, rising in the Taurus, near Argana, runs a sinuous course of more than 300 m., from the river of Zaco to the confluence of the Diallah. In Mesopotamia itself, the two chief rivers are the Khabur and the Hualli, both rising in the Karaja-dagh, and running SE till they join at a place called Nahrain, or 'the Rivers,' whence the combined stream runs S to the Euphrates at Kirkisia.-Of these two streams, the Khabur is the most western, rising 40 geog. m. NW of Rasal-ain, the ancient Resen. It issues with a prodigious force from the ground, being formed, according to the Arabs, of 300 salient springs at the fountain-head. It first runs SE to Ras-al-ain, which is 18 hours, or 60 m. journey, to the S of Merdin. Thence it runs SE to the Hualli, which it joins after a course of more than 200 m. The combined rivers enter the Euphrates 45 m. S of their junction.—The Hualli is the ancient Hermas or Mygdonius of the Greeks, the Saocoras of Ptolemy, and the river of Nisibis or Sengara of Oriental geographers. It is formed by the union of a number of streams coming from the southern slope of the Karaja-dagh, of which the Hermas Proper, and the Mygdonius which waters the plain of Nisibis, are the chief. The former rises 80 m. NW of its junction with the Mygdonius, and the latter 30 m. N of Nisibis. Though the Hermas is the principal stream, yet the Mygdonius is a pretty large stream, and swells to a great size at the annual melting of the snow on Masius. It is probable, however, that as the Hermas itself— which receives all the streams running S from the Masius—must be subject to similar inundations, from the same cause, the waters of the Mygdonius will be dammed up by the superior swell of the main stream, and the exceeding flatness of the plain. At a point 75 m. SE of the junction of the Hualli with the river of Nisibis, the united stream, after describing a large semicircular bend round the mountains of Sinjar to the E, joins the Khabur. The latter river, where it joins the Euphrates, is too deep to be forded, and must be crossed by boats, as was done by the army of Julian, even in the month of April, when the stream was at its lowest. The Hualli anciently, it Hatra, and Tekrit, which in reality is just the line of direction one would suppose it to take from Sinjar, instead of to the SW. In the days of Ptolemy, the Hualli did not join the Chaboras, but fell into the Euphrates by a separate channel—the Masca of Xenophon-five marches E of the mouth of the Araxes or Khabur; whilst the other or larger branch pursued its wonted course to the Tigris. In process of time this branch, the Saocoras, also gradually altered its course more to the W, till it finally joined the Khabur at the present spot. The larger branch continued to run by Hatra to the Tigris, down to times comparatively modern; the bed of its channel is still clearly traceable from the Tigris NW to Hatra. But both these beds are now deserted, and the whole stream of the Hualli now runs SW to the Khabur, instead of SE to the Tigris, by Hatra, and direct S to the Euphrates at Corsote. The extreme flatness of the region through which it anciently ran, and the vast quantity of alluvial matter brought down the stream from the plain of Nisibis, would gradually operate this change, as has been observed in other regions of similar physical character. It was down the Hualli that Trajan conveyed his fleet, built of the timber of the forests of Nisibis, to the Euphrates, but whether by a separate channel, or by those of the Khabur and Hualli collectively, cannot now be determined; the former seems the most probable supposition.—Another stream, of small consequence, ran S of Carræ to the Euphrates, which it entered at the ancient city of Nicephorium or Callinicum, 90 m. W of the mouth of the Khabur. This was anciently called the river of Anthemusia. Towards the N, and the mountains which there divide it from the region of the Upper Tigris, the soil of Mesopotamia is good and productive. Wherever indeed it is well-watered, it is fertile; but the most fertile part of all Mesopotamia was the ancient Mygdonia, called Athemusia by the Greeks, or 'the District of roses,' from the abundance of these flowers which grew here; and at this day the plain of Nisibis is still famed for its white roses. The whole tract of the Upper Khabur and the Hualli is eminently fertile, these being by far the best-watered parts of this region. The whole plain at the foot of the Karajadagh, from the source of the Hualli, and betwixt the course of that stream and the mountain-range, is intersected by innumerable brooks, and presents a continued succession of flat, alluvial meadows, once fertile in rice and grain, now full of ruined towns and villages,—a clear and conclusive proof how much the misery of this country is owing to misgovernment, and how different the yoke of the Roman and Sassanian princes, who formerly shared this large, rich, and productive province, was from that of its present rulers, the indolent and barbarous Osmanlis. Though neither trees nor shrubs now appear in these desolated plains, the immense forests which anciently clothed the southern slopes of Mount Masius, in the vicinity of Nisibis, enabled Trajan to equip a numerous fleet of ships, which he navigated down the stream of the Hualli to the Euphrates. Towards the S and E, the soil progressively deteriorates, till it bears a striking resemblance to that of the Arabian desert: being bare, sterile, and sandy, except on the immediate banks of such scanty streamlets as are found here and there running to the Khabur or the Euphrates. But deserted and barren as the southern part of Mesopotamia now is, it once contained a number of populous towns on both banks of the Euphrates, and on the islands contained in that river, whose inhabitants were supported, partly by the fertility of the soil in their immediate vicinity, and partly by the traffic and business arising out of the passage of caravans, which, in ancient days, when the commerce of the Persian gulf was in its glory, traversed the banks of the Euphrates, on their way to Palmyra, and the head of the Mediterranean. This state of things continued even after the Mahommedan conquest, down to the discovery of the passage to India by the cape of Good Hope, which put an end to this inland commerce and the existence of these cities. At a town called Haditha or Hit, near the Euphrates, are fountains of naphtha or bitumen which anciently supplied the immense quantities of this substance used in constructing the walls and palaces of Babylon. This place is 100 m. to the W of Bagdad; in 33° 43' N lat. To the NE of Hit is a large saline lake which supplies Bagdad with salt. The great number of putrescent lakes and collections of stagnant water which occur in this region, communicate a pestilential quality to the air, heated as it likewise is to an extraordinary degree by the burning sands. It is this air, so heated and so corrupted, which, when set in motion by the want of some equilibrium, produces such fatal effects on animated nature. The samiel or bade simum, as it is called, is more dreaded in Syria and Mesopotamia than in the midst of Arabia itself. It blows from S to N across Mesopotamia, as far as Mosul, and up the Tigris, till its passage is stopped by the mountains to the N. The horses of this region have been renowned from time immemorial. They seldom exceed 14 hands 3 inches in height; but are of exquisite symmetry of form, with delicate limbs, and a fine skin. Kinneir says he once rode a colt of this breed about 90 m. without dismounting; and for a fortnight afterwards he travelled at the rate of 40 m. a-day without losing flesh. A horse of the purest breed will fetch from 1,200 to 3,000 piastres, or from £120 to £300, even at Bagdad. The most common and the most useful animal is the single-humped camel; mules and asses are also used; and buffaloes are kept for their milk. The desert of Mesopotamia is inhabited by the common maned lion, and a species without a mane, by wild asses and antelopes, hyænas, wild boars, bustards and ostriches. The black partridge is found in great abundance on the borders of the rivers and snipes in the marshes. Pelicans are frequently seen on the Euphrates and Tigris. [Kinneir.] Wormwood, as abundant as the heath in some parts of Europe, here covers immense spaces to the exclusion of every other plant; occasionally, however, stripes of agreeable and fertile land, like so many oases, skirt this desert. Tamarinds, wild cherries, cypresses, and weeping-willows, here and there shade the banks of the Euphrates, the waters of which, raised by wheels, are in some districts made to irrigate groves of pomegranates, lemons, and sycamores. The town of Anna is one of these delicious spots. N of Anna, a district covered with mulberries extends along the Euphrates as far as a place called Balis. Here a horde of peaceful Arabs, of the tribe of Beni-Semen, raise silk-worms, and export their produce. This district, almost unknown to Europeans, is called Zombuk by Haji-Khalfa, the Turkish geographer. The caravans which traverse this desert to and from Bassora are compelled to propitiate the Arabs, and have to encounter besides the suffocating samiel,— swarms of locusts, -and the failure of water, - whenever they depart from the line of the river, during a journey of 800 m. from Bassora to Aleppo. Although the descendants of Aram were the aboriginal natives of this region, yet in very early times we find it peopled also by Arabian tribes from the S. As the channel of the Euphrates alone parted Mesopotamia from the Syrian and Arabian deserts, and as it bears on that side a strong resemblance to Arabia, we need not wonder that successive Arab tribes, with their flocks, and herds, and tents, should have crossed the separating stream, to roam in a region so similar to their own. Hence we find Arabs dispersed over all the country, as far as Mount Masius on the N, and the Tigris on the E. All these tribes were by the Greeks collectively denominated Scenitæ, or 'Arabs who dwell in tents.' The district of Osrhoene, in the NW of Mesopotamia, was so called from Osrhoes, the founder of an Arab dynasty of princes, who ruled at Edessa, under the common appellation of Abgarus, till the reign of Caracalla, who, suspecting the fidelity of the last prince, sent him in chains to Rome, and converted this Arab principality into a Roman province. Besides Syrians and Arabs, the pop. was composed of Greeks and Romans. At present it is composed of the original natives, and Greeks-who dwell chiefly in the towns and villages - Turks, Arabs, Kurds, and Yezidis. The Arabs are very numerous, and, together with the Kurds, occupy most of the open country, while the Yezidis inhabit the mountains of Sinjar. The Beni-Melan Arabs occupy the country between Orfa and Merdin. Their numbers are estimated at 50,000 families, and their chief can bring 20,000 horsemen to the field. Another tribe, called the Beni-Ayub, or 'the sons of Job,' are under a chief, who commands 80,000 Arab and Kurdish families who roam in the same tract as the Beni- Melan. This chief is the mightiest potentate of all the nomadic tribes between the Euphrates and Merdin. The Taye Arabs wander in the tract between Nisibis and Mosul, and impose a tribute on all caravans on their way from the former to the latter place. In the month of January, 1847, a caravan of 160 camels, from B. to Aleppo, under the escort of 100 Arabs of the Aghezl tribe, was stopped by the Beduins of the Desert, at Zoar, four days' journey from Aleppo. The sheik of the Beduins sent word to the Aleppo merchants to whom the goods were addressed, that he had stopped the caravan, and required them to come and ransom their merchandise for 20,000 piastres. The owners of the caravan consulted with the pasha; and after some fruitless attempts to make an arrangement with the sheik, more accordant with the dignity of the pasha, and attended with less risk to the persons of the merchants, two small Turkish traders set out for Zoar with the required sum. Of the camels 125 were laden with Persian tobacco, 34 with dates, and one with Persian silk. When the merchants arrived at the sheik's tent they were required immediately to pay down their 20,000 piastres, as an indispensable preliminary to business: having done this, they were met by the unsatisfactory intelligence that of the 160 loads only 46 remained, the rest having been dispersed through the desert in different directions. Bitter and violent were the exclamations of the merchants against the bad faith of the sheik, but their exertions to recover a portion of the ransom paid upon the understanding that all the loads were to be restored were ineffectual. At last they departed on their return to Aleppo with their 46 loads. But they had not proceeded a day upon their journey with this diminished caravan before they were attacked by another tribe of Beduins, who took possession of their persons and camels. The sheik of this fresh tribe sent, as the other sheik had done, a message to the pasha, declaring that he had seized the caravan, and should detain the two merchants and their goods, until the person of one of their sheiks, of high rank, whom the pasha detained as a hostage for their good behaviour, was given up and restored to them. After some disputing, finding the Arabs stick to their demand, the pasha, unable to get the better of them by force, consented to deliver up the sheik into the keeping of the mutsellim, who is in the habit of treating with the Beduins, and well-known to them. The whole country from Merdin to Karkisia, along the Khabur and Hermas, has of late been overrun by the Jerbah tribe from Arabia; and the vicinity of Bagdad itself is infested by the Delaim and Jubbur tribes. The Yezidis are a ferocious, sanguinary, and numerous race, dwelling partly in Kurdistan, and partly in the mountains of Sinjar. In Kurdistan they possess several districts between Jezirah and the source of the Susan branch of the Tigris. Kinneir says that about 10,000 families of them inhabit these districts. They also inhabit several villages on the road from Zako to Mosul; but their chief residence is in the mountainous region of Sinjar. Their language is the Kurdish. Kinneir estimates the number of this daring sect at 2,000,000 souls; while a French traveller estimates them at only one-tenth of that number.—In such a motley pop. composed of Turks, Arabs, Kurds, and Syrians, many different languages and dialects must be found. The purest dialect of the Aramean language is and always has been spoken in Mesopotamia; at Edessa, or Orfa, Kurdish and Arabic are spoken among the wandering tribes, so called; at Beer and Orfa, Turkish is spoken; at Mosul and Merdin, Arabic and Kurdish, with Turkish and the vulgar Chaldee. In ancient days, Mesopotamia contained a vast number of towns and cities all celebrated both in sacred and profane history; but of these only a few remain at this day. For more than 700 years this region was the battle-ground of political strife between the powers of the West and the East; and yet, during all that long period, it was populous and flourishing. But since it fell under Turkish domination it has gradually though rapidly declined in pop. and fertility. Descending the Euphrates to its entrance on the plains of Mesopotamia and Syria, the first place of political importance, as a ford from Syria into the former region, was Zeugma, the ordinary place where the Romans crossed their armies into Mesopotamia, along a strong stone-bridge protected by a castle on the Mesopotamian side of the river, named Seleucia, and connected with the city of Apamea. Both the cities and the castle are now in ruins; but the remains of the bridge are still visible. Zeugma is supposed by D'Anville to correspond with the modern Rum Kala, or 'castle of Rum,' or 'of the Romans;' but this is a mistake, as the latter place stands higher up the river, and is a modern building compared with Zeugma. About 20 geog. m. below Rum Kala, in about 36° 59' N lat., is Bir or Beer, a city of some importance, having a castle commanding the pass over the river. According to Buckingham, who visited this place in 1816, it contained 400 houses, and 4,000 inhabitants; but according to Wolff, who passed through it on his way to Palestine in 1824, there are more than 15,000 Turkish inhabitants alone, besides a few Armenian Christians. Going S along the Euphrates, and then E as far as the mouth of the Khabur, we meet with little but ruins of former cities and towns which flourished here in the days of Rome and Parthia. Kerkisia, at the junction of the Khabur with the Euphrates, represents the Carchemish of Scripture, taken by Pharaoh- Necho, and recovered by Nebuchadnezzar. This was always a place of political importance, being the frontier-garrison of the Romans, and the successors of Constantine, against the Parthians, and their successors the Persians. It was fortified by Dioclesian, and was ordinarily furnished with a garrison of 10,000 men.—At a considerable distance to the W is the ancient Thapsacus, on the Syrian side of the Euphrates, famous as a pass over the Euphrates, and corresponding to the Tiphsah of Scripture, the extreme limit of Solomon's kingdom to the NE. This ford is on the direct road from Damascus and Palestine to Upper Mesopotamia and the Tigris. Here the Euphrates is 800 yards broad, but shallow at low water. -Beyond the Khabur, along the Euphrates, are the towns of Meschid Rahabah, Rahabah Melic, and Anna, the ancient Annathon of Ammianus, the residence then as now of an independent Arabian emir. It is situated on an island in the river, and is composed of two long streets, walled and fortified .- Thilutha, farther E, on the same river, was an impregnable fortress which defied the arms of Julian.-We have no modern towns of note on the Euphrates till we arrive at Feluja, all the ancient towns having perished. Feluja is a little to the SE of the ancient Anbar, once the capital of the district of Ancobarilis, and the seat of the Abasside khalifs before the foundation of Bagdad. Anbar is now in ruins; but Feluja is a place of some importance, being placed at the junction of the Euphrates, and a canal connecting it with the Tigris at Bagdad, from which Feluja is only 12 leagues distant. These are all the places of note on the southern side of Mesopotamia at present. The other ancient towns of Oropus, Sura, Dura, Necarda, Rakka, Nicephorium or Callinicum, Chaboras, and Pombiditha, have all disappeared.—In interior Mesopotamia the first city of note is the modern Orfa, the seat of a pashalik, and one of the finest cities in Asiatic Turkey. This city is the ancient Edessa, the capital for three centuries of the principality of Osrhoene, and afterwards of Roman Mesopotamia. It lies two days' journey E of the Euphrates, and 67 m. NE of Bir. D'Anville places it at the source of the Scirtas; but he is wrong in fixing its latitude in 36°, for its true position is 37° 10′ N lat. To the S of Orfa, 8 hours' journey, is Haran, famous as the abode of Abraham after he left Ur or Orfa, and celebrated in Roman history for the defeat of Crassus by the Parthians.—This place is now in ruins. Considerably to the E of Orfa is the noted city and fortress of Merdin, the ancient Miredis or Marde. Its pop., according to Kinneir, is 11,000, and according to Buckingham 20,000, two-thirds of whom are Mahommedans. There are besides 1,000 families of Shemsuahs, or worshippers of the sun, who are politically included amongst the flock of the Syrian patriarch.—Between Merdin and Diarbekir the country is high and hilly, the hills being at least 1,000 ft. above the level of the plain of Merdin, and the intervening valleys 500 ft. above the same level.—From Nisibis to Mosul, a space of 155 m. in a direct line, nothing occurs worthy of notice. The ancient Tissaphalta is recognised in the village of Sefaya; but the Persian fortress of Ur has ceased to exist.—Mosul, a large and ancient city on the r. bank of the Tigris, still survives amidst surrounding desolation and solitude. Its pop. is reckoned by the inhabitants themselves at 100,000; but by Buckingham, in 1816, at less than half that number. II. We shall now briefly sketch the SE division of the pashalik, or Irak-Arabi, and that part of ancient Assyria to the SE of the Little Zab. This is an extensive, though now a comparatively deserted and neglected region, chiefly possessed by hordes of wandering Arabs. Its limit on the W of the Tigris, may be traced by a line drawn from Feluja on the Euphrates, to the Tigris opposite Bagdad; and on the E of the latter stream, at the junction of the Tigris and Little Zab. By the former line it is separated from Mesopotamia; and by the latter from the NW part of Assyria and Kurdistan. It comprehends the whole tract to the S of the Euphrates, from the junction of the former with the Khabur,-to the Arab town of Koniet or Grain, a little to the SW of the mouth of the Shat-al-Arab, -all the intermediate region between the Euphrates and Tigris, from Feluja and Bagdad, to Kornah at the junction of these rivers, and on the E of the Tigris from the mouth of the Little Zab. This region extends NE and E to the Kurdistan mountains and the Aiagha-dagh, the ancient Zagros, and SE to the Synne river, or river of Mendeli, which enters the Tigris at Jarjariya. From this junction a small stripe of narrow alluvial land, between the Tigris and the Hamrin hills, stretching to the mouths of the Karun, is nominally included within this tract in modern maps; but in ancient times the boundary in this direction was much more distinct and specific, the Gyndes or the modern Hud, being the boundary towards Susiana or Khuzistan. So numerous have been the physical and political revolutions to which this once-renowned region has been subjected in modern times, that it is wholly impossible to assign it definite political limits. The whole tract to the E of the Tigris, from near Bagdad to the Persian gulf, is in the hands of warlike Arab tribes, alike independent of the Turks and Persians; and the same is the case to the W of the Euphrates, all the way from Bassora to Feluja. The Turkish power is restricted in effect to the few remaining towns on the banks of one or other of these rivers and their tributary streams. This region may be divided into three parts: Chaldæa,-Babylonia,-and that part which lies beyond the Tigris. The first of these lay to the W and S of the Euphrates; the second was the ancient district of Babylonia or Babylon, and included all the tract between the two rivers, from the uppermost of the intersecting canals down to Korna, being the most fertile division of the whole; the last division was never included in the second, but belonged to ancient Assyria. In other words, the modern Irak-Arabi comprehends the western division anciently called Chaldæa, and the middle division called Babylonia; and the eastern part of the pashalik of Bagdad corresponds to the third division. There is no part of Asiatic Turkey, the interior geography of which is so little known as that of this region. The Greeks,-who, by their conquest of this extensive region under Alexander, and their subsequent possession of it for more than a century and a half under the Seleucidæ, had of all others the best opportunity of knowing and describing it,— have left us next to nothing on the subject; and during the ascendancy of Roman power it was under Parthian domination, so that their writers could describe it only from such meagre reports as they now and then obtained from the journals of some itinerant merchant or member of a caravan. Ptolemy's description of it is comprised in one quarto page, including the positions of 27 places, few of which—Babylon excepted—can now be recognised, so completely have they all perished from the page of history. The Divine malediction seems not only to have been verified on Babylon itself, but on the whole surrounding region also; so that what was once the residence of a powerful and wealthy people, -filled with numerous and populous cities and villages, -the abode of civilization, agriculture, manufactures, and commerce, -where temples and trophies reared their heads in every direction,—is now a sterile desert, except on the immediate banks of the Euphrates, where here and there a village, or a camp of wandering Arabs, may perchance be met with! BAGDAD. The city of Bagdad, the cap. of the above pash., whilst it enjoyed the splendid presence of the court of the khalifate, was the most celebrated city in Western Asia; and this honour it held for more than five centuries, during the sway of the house of Al-Abbas; but compared with many other Oriental cities, its origin is modern, having been built in 762 by the khalif Al-Mansur, the second prince of the Abasside dynasty, who gave his new city the appellation of Dar-al-Salum, or 'the Gate of peace.' It has been erroneously supposed by some to occupy the same spot on which Babylon formerly stood; the mistake may have originated in the circumstance of its being built upon the site of Seleucia, which was frequently styled New Babylon. Bagdad is indeed the last of a succession of magnificent cities, which were built at different periods in the same extensive plain, and each of which was raised from the ruins of its predecessor. Babylon was exhausted of its inhabitants and its ornaments by the city Seleucia; Seleucia, again, was supplanted by Ctesiphon; which, in its turn, yielded to Almadayen; and, last of all, Bagdad supplied a residence to the sovereigns of the East. It has, however, sadly declined from its early magnitude and grandeur. It was originally built on both sides of the Tigris, here 620 ft. wide, and extended several miles along the river; but it is now chiefly confined to the eastern bank, to which there is access by a bridge of boats. The rivers Tigris and Euphrates approach so near each other in this quarter, that the intervening distance is only 6 hours' walk, and consists of one immense fertile meadow which extends all the way to Korna; but the actual confluence of these great streams is 200 m. direct distance below B., and 300 m. above the point where the united stream enters the Persian gulf. A modern canal, called the canal of Isa, connects the two rivers between Feluja and B. As the level of the Euphrates is here higher than that of the Tigris, its surplus waters, during inundation, are discharged into the latter by means of this canal. B. is surrounded with a wall of brick, which bears obvious marks of different eras of construction and repair; the oldest part,—as in most Mahommedan works,—being the best, and the newest, the worst. This wall has large round towers at the principal angles, with turrets, at small distances from each other, in the intermediate spaces between the large angular towers. On these last are batteries planted with brass cannon of different calibres, badly mounted, and only 50 in number, including those of all the fortifications towards the land-side of the city. The circumf. of the wall, including both sides of the river, is about 5 m. [Kinneir.] The whole wall has a ditch of considerable depth around it, but it is merely an excavation without masonry or lining of any kind. The gates are six in number. A large portion of the city is destitute of buildings, particularly on the NE side; and even where the houses abound, near the river, a profusion of trees is seen; so that, on taking a view of the whole city from a lofty terrace, it seems to rise out of a grove of palms. The houses are all of furnace-burnt bricks, of a yellowish colour, and small size. The streets are narrow and unpaved; so narrow that two horsemen meeting can hardly pass [Kinneir]; their sides generally present two blank walls, -windows being rarely ever seen opening on the public thoroughfare, while the doors entering to the interior dwellings are small and mean; indeed, with the exception of some tolerably regular lines of bazaars, and a few open squares, B. may be denominated a labyrinth of alleys and passages. The palace of the pasha stands at the NW angle of the city, near the river; it is rather an extensive than a grand building. B. has more than 100 mosques, of which only 30 can be distinguished by their minarets; there are upwards of 30 caravanserais, 50 public baths, and numerous bazaars; but in architecture, beauty, and convenience, these buildings are decidedly inferior to those of Orfa, Damascus, and Cairo. B. is entirely supplied with water, by sakas or water-carriers, from the river. These sakas are very numerous. The pop. is variously estimated by travellers at from 50,000 to 80,000 souls. In 1652, Tavernier estimated it at 15,000; in 1779, it was computed to be 100,000. Buckingham estimated it at 80,000, of whom 50,000 were Arabs; the remainder are Chaldeans, Christians, Armenians, Turks, Persians, Jews, Indians, and black slaves. In the year 1831, the plague, combined with a sudden inroad of the river, reduced the pop. from 75,000 to about 25,000. The river on this occasion destroyed whole quarters of the city at once, and in one night 15,000 persons perished. [Skinner.] The chief officers of the civil and military departments are of Osmanli families from Constantinople, though they are themselves mostly natives of the place. The mercantile classes are almost all of Arabian descent; the lower ranks are a medley of Turks, Arabs, Persians, and Hindus. The commerce of B. chiefly consists of Indian manufactures and produce, which are brought up the Tigris from Bengal, by the port of Bassora, and distributed into the Nejed country, through Syria, and over Kurdistan, Armenia, and Asia Minor. Some of the caravans from B. to Syria have been known to consist of 5,000 camels, each carrying a quarter of a ton of goods, at an average charge of 100 piastres per kintal, or about £20 per ton. The trade of Aleppo and Damascus with B.,—the former through the desert, across the Euphrates at Anna or Hit,—and the latter across the desert, by way of Tadmor, also traversing the Euphrates at Hit,—is chiefly carried on by Mussulmans and Christians: some few Europeans are engaged in it, but hitherto British merchants have seldom adventured on it. There is not often more than one caravan of from 700 to 1,000 camels annually between Aleppo and B., and one of from 1,000 to 1,200 or 1,500 yearly between Damascus and B. The articles generally sent from both those places to B. are chiefly cotton twist, calicoes, shirtings, prints, imitation shawls, woollen cloths, and dyes, and are received from Europe generally. The chief articles received in return, independently of remittances in specie,—which generally form the principal return,— are Persian and Hussineeh tombag, galls, buffalo hides, East India indigo, pearls, Cashmere shawls, some Mocha coffee, and an assortment of small articles. The cost of carriage varies from 350 to 450 piastres per cantar between Aleppo and B., and from 350 to 400 piastres per cantar between Damascus and B. The chief cause which enhances the price of carriage across the desert is the insecurity of the route from the Arab tribes, which—as we have seen—frequently obliges the body of merchants at B. to compromise and give considerable sums and presents to the chiefs of the Shammahk and Anazee tribes at B., who become responsible for their respective hordes not plundering the caravan on its passage through their districts. These presents vary according to the value of the caravan. The Aghali Arabs also, who act as guards, and accompany the caravan, receive presents, and are paid as a kind of military and protecting escort. It is stated, on the authority of information obtained from the chief merchants interested in the trade between Damascus and B., that the trade of the former has considerably suffered from the declining state of the B. market, arising from Persia no longer supplying herself to the extent it formerly did from B.; Persia now receiving her chief supplies, in the northern provinces, via Trebisond, from Great Britain and Constantinople, and the southern direct from India and the Persian gulf. [Consul Werry.] This commerce was said to be increasing in 1816, under the moderation of the existing pasha. By those best informed on the subject, it was affirmed that nowhere in the Turkish dominions were the people so little oppressed, and commerce so little subjected to restraint, as here; but in later years, through the increasing poverty of the Turkish government, oppression has increased, and commerce has been anew subjected to vexatious exactions; while the Arabs have become more daring in their predatory exploits. In addition to this, the Persian commerce has greatly declined, since the Persians have found the route of Erzerum and Tokat to be more safe and easy than that of B. to Constantinople. The goods formerly deposited here, as in a central situation, are now carried straight to the Turkish capital by that route, to the greater profit of the original Persian dealer, and to the corresponding loss of the B. merchant, through whose hands they formerly passed. B. was once famous for its Oriental literature, which was carefully fostered here under the genial sunshine of the khalifs; but it has long since suffered a total eclipse. Since the extinction of the khalifate by the Tartars, Arabian learning is now at so low an ebb, that there is not a single collection of good books to be found in all the city, nor any individual mollah distinguished by his proficiency in the learning of his country. Not a perfect copy of the 'Arabian Nights' Entertainments' is now to be found in all Bagdad, though the famed capital of Harun-al- Raschid, who makes so great a figure in that celebrated work, with his vizier Giafar. The climate of B. would be healthy enough but for the great heats which prevail in summer, the therm. standing in the shade, from the latter end of July till the middle of August, at 119° and 122° at noon; in 1819 the heat was so great as to reach 150° of Fahrenheit in the shade. During these intense heats great numbers even of the natives die; and the English staying occasionally here during the hot months, after a long residence in India, sigh for the temperate region of Bengal or Hindostan. The lat. of Bagdad, as observed by Buckingham, was 33° 18′ 57" N; and by Rich 33° 19′ 40"; Kinneir places it in 33° 20'; Rich fixed its long. in 44° 45' 45" E, Kinneir in 44° 24'. It is 540 m. from Tabriz. History.] Almansor, the second caliph of the family of the Abassides, having disgusted by his cruelties the inhabitants of Hasomia, and given occasion to conspiracies against his life, resolved to abandon a place which was so determined in its disaffection, and to remove the seat of the empire to a city founded by himself. The following account is given by the Persian writers of the foundation of the new city, and the origin of its name. Khosru, named Amishirwan, had given the plain on which B. now stands to one of his wives, who built and dedicated there a chapel or oratory to her favourite idol Bagh; and from this circumstance the whole of the neighbouring district was called Baghdad, i. e. in the Persian language, 'the gift of Bagh.' This little temple, in process of time, served as a place of retreat to a hermit of extraordinary sanctity, who happened to meet with Almansor, while he was riding on the banks of the Tigris, and meditating on his new scheme; and who, upon learning from one of the attendants the design of the caliph, mentioned an ancient tradition that a city was to be built in that place by a person named Moclas. Almansor on being informed of the hermit's observation, declared that the name of Moclas had been given to him by his nurse; gave thanks to God for having destined him to be the author of so great a work; and instantly fixed upon the spot where he stood as the situation of his intended capital. According to Arabian authors, however, there was none of these marvellous and romantic occurrences in the caliph's proceedings. The ground was deliberately chosen near the confluence of the Euphrates and the Tigris, as being a favourable situation both for the defence of the city and for the conveyance of provisions, and it was called Bagdad, i. e. 'the garden of Dad,' because a Christian monk, of the name of Dad, had resided on the spot where it was built. The first part of the city was situated on the western bank of the Tigris; and was of a circular form, with the caliph's palace and the great mosque in the centre. The city was completed in the 149th year of the Hegira, (A. D. 766,) and received the name of Medinat-al-Salam, 'the City of peace,' alluding either to the name of Jerusalem, or to the quiet which prevailed throughout the empire at the time when it was finished, or to the name which was frequently applied by the Orientals to the river Tigris, - Wadi-al-Salam, 'the Torrent of peace.' But whatever was the reason of this appellation, it was generally adopted by the neighbouring nations; and hence the city of Bagdad is often called by the Greeks, Irenopolis, which is equivalent to the Arabic, Medinat-al-Salam. The city, when completed, was of a circular form, enclosed with a double wall, BAG and defended by a considerable number of towers. Its gates were disposed in such a manner that those of the first wall were in a sloping or oblique direction with respect to those of the second; and from this circumstance it was sometimes called by the Arabians, Zaura, i. e. 'the Crooked.' In the centre of the whole was the castle, or citadel, which commanded every part of the town; and a bridge was constructed over the Tigris, to facilitate the communication between the two divisions of the city. B. was much enlarged and beautified by succeeding caliphs; but particularly by Mostanser Billa, who founded here a famous college, which was remarkable for the elegance of its structure, the greatness of its revenues, and the number of its students. It contained also several other well-endowed colleges, was renowned for the elegance of the Arabic spoken within its walls; and produced a greater number of learned men, than any other place in the Mahommedan dominions, except Mecca and Medina. The city of B. continued to be the seat of the caliphs of the house of Abbas, and the capital of the Moslem empire, for the space of more than 500 years. During this long period it sustained several obstinate sieges, and was the scene of many a bloody revolution. In 812, it was attacked by Al Mamon, the second son of Harun Al Raschid, who had rebelled against his elder brother Al Amin; and in a siege of twelve months, almost the whole eastern part of the city was laid in ruins. In 945 it was afflicted by so severe a famine, that many persons were punished for feeding upon the flesh of children; and this dreadful scarcity was followed by a pestilence which swept away great numbers of the inhabitants. In 1026 it was besieged by a numerous army of Turks, who plundered, and set it on fire. In 1055 it was pillaged by the troops of Trogrud, or Togrol Beck, who assumed to himself the office of Emir-al-omra, and made great encroachments upon the power of the caliphs. At length, in the 656th of the Hegira, (A. D. 1258,) B. was taken by Hulaku, or Hulagou, the grandson of Zengis; its reigning prince, Al Mostasom Billah, put to death; the caliphate itself abolished; and the city given up to pillage and massacre for the space of seven days. It remained in the possession of the Tartars or Moguls till 1393, when it was taken by Timur Beg, or Tamerlane the Great, from Sultan Ahmed Ebn Weis, who abandoned his capital to the conqueror, and took refuge in the territories of the Greek emperor; but found means to regain the city in a short time, and to keep possession till 1400. It was then attacked a second time by Timur, and vigorously defended by Ahmed's governor Farruj; at the end of forty days, however, it was taken by assault, the inhabitants barbarously massacred, and the principal building levelled with the ground. From this period, it was alternately in the hands of Sultan Ahmed, of Abubekr, grandson of Tamerlane, and of Kara Yusef the Turkoman, till 1412, when it was secured by the last mentioned prince, and remained in the possession of his descendants till 1470. It was then occupied by Usun Cassan, in whose family it continued till 1510, when it was taken by Shah Ismael, surnamed Sofi; and, for 120 years after, it was the object of perpetual contest between the Turks and Persians. In 1638, it was taken by Amurath, or Morad IV.; and given up to pillage by the cruel conqueror. From this period B. has declined in extent and magnificence.—Kinneir.—Niebuhr.—Fraser.—Ainsworth.
BAGE'-LE-CHATEL, or BAUGE', a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Bourg.—The cant. comprises 11 com. The com. is 18 m. NW of Bourg. Pop. 740.
BAGE'-LA-VILLE, a commune of France, 2 m. NE of the above. Pop. 2,017.
BAGENDAISK, a town in Siberia, in the hilly country which separates the basins of the Angara and the Lena, at an alt. of 524 ft. above sea-level.
BAGERA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Bagdad, 60 m. SE of Orfa.

BAGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, 4 m. S of Narbonne, on an etang or small lake which communicates with that of Sigean. Pop. 875.—Also a com. in the dep. of Pyrénées-Orientales, cant. of Thuir, celebrated for its artesian wells. BAGGAS, BACCAS, or BACKAS, a peculiar tribe inhabiting the exterior Mangrove islands, on the W coast of Africa, from Nunez as far as the Isles de Los. Caillie was informed that the B. are successful cultivators of the soil as compared with most of their neighbours; that they plough their fields somewhat in the European manner, raise large quantities of rice, and manufacture salt and palm oil. They are quite black, with woolly hair, which they shave away on the forehead, but let grow at the back. They pass a copper ring through the cartilage of the nose by way of ornament; but the only article of clothing which they employ is a strip of calico wound round the waist and between the thighs.
BAGGINTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 31 m. S by E of Coventry. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 245.
BAGGRA, a range of mountains, or rather a bleak table-land, in co. Cork, between the basin of the Blackwater and the Lee. It extends from E to W above 10 m., and has a breadth of 6 m.
BAGGY POINT, a bluff head on the coast of Devonshire, 4 m. NNE of Barnstaple, off which is a dangerous reef called Baggy Leap.

BAGH, or BHAG, a town of Beluchistan, in Cutch Gundava, near the entrance from Sinde to the Bolan pass, in N lat. 28° 56'. Masson estimated it to contain from 600 to 800 houses in 1826; but it had declined considerably at that period from its former flourishing condition. It enjoys a considerable transit trade, and the monopoly of the trade in sulphur from mines near Sanni. It is situated near the river Nari.
BAGHAW, a town of Affghanistan, in Sistan, in N lat. 30° 16'. Pop. 2,000.
BAGHERIA, a town of Sicily, in the district of Palermo. Pop. 4,000.
BAGHERMI. See BAGARMEH.
BAGHIRATI. See BHAGHIRETTI.
BAGHWAN, or BAGWANA, a village of Beluchistan, 70 m. S of Kelat, in a fertile valley producing pomegranates, grapes, apricots, figs, and melons; but extremely cold in winter from its great elevation.
BAGHWANAH, a town in the Punjab, near the W bank of the Sutledge, in N lat. 31° 13'. Pop. 15,000.—Von Hugel.

BAGILT-FAWR, a township in the p. of Holywell in Flintshire, on the estuary of the Dee, 25 m. from Holywell. Pop. with Fechan, 2,150. There are extensive collieries, and lead, iron, and alkali works here.
BAGLAH, a large village of Anatolia, near Za'faran Boli.
BAGLAN, a parish in Glamorganshire, 4 m. S by E of Neath. Pop. 548.
BAGLI, a town of Turkish Armenia, 15 m. SW of Kars, in N lat. 40° 35'.—Also a town of Lower Egypt, on the Rosetta branch of the Nile, in N lat. 30° 39′.
BAGNA, a town of Turkey, in the sanjak of Sophia, on the Maritza, 45 m. SE of Sophia.

BAGNACAVALLO, a town of Italy, in the Statesof-the-Church, in the legation and 36 m. SSE of Ferrara, and 12 m. W of Ravenna, on the Senio. It is a walled town, and handsomely built; and possesses some silk spinning-mills. Its principal articles of commerce are silk and hemp. Large quantities of the latter are cultivated in the environs. It was the birth-place of the painter Romenghi. Pop. 4,000.

BAGNA DI ACQUA, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. of Pisa, 3 m. SSE of Lari, and 17 E of Leghorn. It is situated near the base of Monte Parlascio; and is divided into two parts, the upper of which is named Petraja, and the lower Parlascio. It has been long celebrated for its thermal waters and baths.
BAGNAGAR. See HYDRABAD.
BAGNAJA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 3 m. NE of Viterbo. It has a great annual cattle-fair.
BAGNALL, a chapelry in the p. of Stoke-upon- Trent, Staffordshire, 6 m. SW of Leeke. Pop., with the townships of Bucknall, Eaves, and Ubberley, 1,609.
BAGNALSTOWN, a small town in co. Carlow, 7½ m. S of Carlow, on the river Barrow. Pop. 2,225. Granite for building and flag-stone are extensively quarried in the vicinity.

BAGNA'RA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the legation and 20 m. W of Ravenna, and 5 m. SW of Lugo, on the r. bank of the Santerno. Pop. 600.—Also a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 2da, in the district and 15 m. NNE of Reggio, and 4 m. ENE of Scylla. It possesses a small seaport, and has considerable commerce in wine and pitch. Excellent Muscat wine is produced in the environs. BAG The town was to a great extent destroyed by the great earthquake in 1783. Pop. 3,175.

BAGNARE'A, a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the deleg. and 17 m. N of Viterbo. It is situated on the E declivity of Monte Bolsena, on the E of the lake of that name. It is the see of a bishopric, and was the birth-place of Buonaventura. Pop. 3,000.
BAGNASCO, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 12 m. ESE of Mondovi, on the l. bank of the Tanaro.
BAGNEAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, and cant. of Nemours. It has fine glass-works. Pop. 217.

BAGNE'RES - EN - BIGORRE, or BAGNE'RESADOUR, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées.- The arrond. comprises an area of 188,551 hectares, and possessed in 1836 a pop. of 93,046; and in 1846, 95,815. It contains 10 cant., viz., Arreau, Bagneresen-Bigorre, Borderes, Campan, Castelnau-Magnoac, La Barthe-de-Neste, Lannemezan, Mauléon-Barousse, Nestier, and Vielle-Aure.—The cant. comprises 18 com.—The town of B., 12 m. SSE of Tarbes, and 35 m. SE of Pau, occupies a beautiful situation on the l. bank of the Adour, at the entrance of the valley of Campan, at an alt. of 1,600 ft. above sea-level. It is encircled by richly-wooded heights, which rise in gradual undulations towards the mountain-acclivities that terminate in the lofty snow-clad Pic-du-Midi, alt. 9,538 ft., which commands one of the noblest and most extensive views in Europe. It is generally well-built; the houses are modern and commodious, and the streets-which are watered by numerous rivulets from the Adourare broad and well-made. The principal buildings, besides the great thermal establishment, are the parish-church, the hospital, the market-place, and the theatre. There are also a communal college, a board-of-trade, a reading-room, museums, &c., &c. The promenades are numerous and diversified. That named the Salut, which commences near the centre of the town, and is lined for three quarters of a mile with stately trees, is the most frequented. - The mineral springs of B. are the saline, 22 in number, and all within the limits of the town; the ferruginous, a single spring a \( \frac{1}{2} \) m. distant from the town; and a sulphureous, in the valley of Trebons. They all exhibit considerable diversity of ingredients and temperature. One spring is reported to have a temp. of 144°; others are so low as 90°; and they do not all maintain an unvarying temp. Some are simply saline, with small quantities of iron.—The climate of B. is remarkably equable; and is sedative in its influence upon consumptive invalids, but depressing to the healthy. It is more humid, and its range of temperature is lower than that of Pau, averaging about 64° of Fah. from June to October.—The principal manufactures of B. are woollen and linen fabrics, bolting-cloth, caddis, netting, paper, and fine cutlery. There are besides extensive marble-works, saw-mills, dye-works, tanneries, and printing establishments. In the environs are quarries of the finest limestone and slate. Two fairs are held here annually.—This town is of the most remote antiquity. Tradition dates its foundation as early as 7 centuries before the building of Rome. On the Roman conquest of Aquitania, it became the head-quarters of government; and numerous monuments still exist to attest its importance as the Pyrenean metropolis of the conquerors. Under Augustus it was the Aquensis vicus. In the Itinerary of Antoninus it is called Aquæ Convenarum. We have historical information that in the 10th cent. it was surrounded by strongly fortified walls, with ramparts and drawbridges. The town contains a regular pop. of about P 8,000; but during the season—which commences in June, and ends in October-it becomes the annual resort of visitors to the number of 4,000 and upwards. - Taylor on the Climate of Pau. - Millin .- Pambrun, Bagnères de Bigorre, et les environs, 8vo.

BAGNE'RES-DE-LUCHON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, and arrond. of St. Gaudens. The cant. comprises 31 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,773.— The town occupies an elevated site in an extensive and fertile valley of the Pyrenees,-"an extensive basin encompassed by fir-covered mountains" [Paris], -watered by the Pique and two tributary streams, whose confluent waters fall into the Neste, 4 m. from the frontiers of Spain, and 24 m. SSW of St. Gaudens. It is celebrated for its mineral waters; and during the season—from May to October—is much resorted to. A beautiful avenue adorned with rows of elm-trees, and bordered with well-built houses, leads to the baths. "None of the baths," says Inglis, "can boast of such perfect shade as B. de Luchon." A handsome establishment on the mountains to the E of the town contains 12 springs resembling those of Bareges and Cauterets, of which 6 are thermal. They all contain sulphur, salt, soda, bitumen, and an insoluble matter whose principle has not been ascertained. Several ancient votive altars and baths indicate that this locality also was known to the Romans under the name of Aquæ balneariæ Lixonienses. Excellent chocolate is manufactured in the town. In the environs are extensive quarries of slate and lime, and a copper-mine, and near the summit of the port d'Ov, a rich vein of lead still unwrought. Pop. 2,077. - Fontan sur les Eaux thermales de Luchon. 1843.—Inglis's Switzerland. Chap. xxxiii.

BAGNES (VAL DE), a valley of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, watered by an affluent of the Dranse, which separates at St. Branchier from the valley of Entremont, and runs first in a W direction, then curving to the SE, terminates in the great glaciers of Chermontane. One of its lofty passes, the Col des Fenetres [alt. 9,000 ft.] is that by which Calvin fled in 1541 from persecution in Aosta. It is fertile; and contains mines of argentiferous lead, copper, cobalt, asbestos, &c. The village from which the valley takes its name is situated on a small stream 4 m. above its confluence with the Dranse, and 6 m. ESE of Martigny, at an alt. of 2,621 ft. above sea-level. It contains several mineral springs and baths. Pop. of the parish, which comprises the entire valley, 3,961.—In 1818, the ice beneath the glacier of Gétroz which lies towards the head of the valley, upon its E side, accumulated so much as to form a lake, by the stoppage of the Dranse, halfa-league long, 700 ft. wide, and in one part 200 ft. deep. An attempt was made to drain it by a canal cut through the ice; but it failed, and a deluge of 500,000,000 cubic feet of water swept through a valley full of defiles with irresistible force and enormous velocity,—the fall from Getroz to Chatre being 2,800 ft. The torrent reached Martigny, 24 m. distant, in 11 hour; and entered the lake of Geneva within 7 hours of its bursting forth. The lake itself was emptied in 1 hour.
BAGNEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Sceaux, 3 m. S of Paris. It has quarries of freestone. Pop. 885.
BAGNEUX-LA-FOSSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aube, cant. of Les Riceys. It produces excellent red wines. Pop. 788.
BAGNI-DI-CASCIANA, or Bagno-A-Acqua, a village of Tuscany, 6 m. SE of Lara, on the Casciana, Pop. 1,012. There are hot salino-ferruginous baths here of a temp. of 97° 30'.

BAGNI-DI-PISA, or Bagni-a-San-Guilliano, a BAG village of Tuscany, 5 m. NE of Pisa, on the E flank of Monte-Pisano. Pop. 575. It is celebrated for its effervescing thermal springs, and for its baths which were re-established in the 12th cent. on the site of the Aquæ Pisanæ of the Romans.
BAGNI-DI-LUCCA. See BAGNO.
BAGNI-A-MORBA, a village of Tuscany, 15 m. S of Volterra, and 30 WSW of Sienna. It has thermal springs and baths.

BAGNISKA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Bosnia, in the sanjak and 25 m. SE of Novibazar, at the opening of a narrow gorge, traversed by a tributary of the Metrovitza. It has a castle, and in the vicinity is a ferruginous spring.—Also a river which unites with the Ibar about 9 m. below Novibazar. It occupies only a small extent of the rocky channel which it has worn for itself.

BAGNO, or Bagno-a-Corsena, a village of Lucca, 14 m. N of the town of Lucca, on the r. bank of the Lima, 3 m. above the confluence of that river with the Serchio. At this village are the principal and most ancient of the celebrated baths of Lucca, which have been resorted to since the 12th cent. Near the Bagno-Rosso, or the Docce-Basse, and the Bagni-alla-Villa, are the Ducal palace, and a theatre. Pop. 780.

BAGNO-IN-ROMAGNA, a town of Tuscany, on a ramification of the Apennino-della-Penna, and on the l. bank of the Savio, 38 m. E of Florence. There are several thermal springs here which were known to the Romans, and well-frequented baths. Pop. 822.

BAGNOLES, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Orne, and communes of Conterne and Tessé-la-Madeleine. It has hot sulphureous springs, and cold ferruginous springs, and much-frequented baths. The military bath establishment here is very commodious.

BAGNOLET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Pantin, 3 m. NE of Paris. It contains numerous handsome villas, and has quarries of gypsum. Peaches are extensively cultivated in this locality. Pop. 1,099.

BAGNOLI, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 15 m. S of Padua. Pop. 2,742.—Also a town of Naples, in the cant. and 9 m. SW of Trevento. It is built on the side of a hill, and contains 5 churches, an abbey, and an hospital. Pop. 4,379. BAGNOLI, BAGNOLO, or BAGNUOLI, a town of Naples, in the Principato-Ultra, in the district and 18 m. SW of San-Angelo-de'-Lombardi. It possesses a fine church. Pop. 4,794.
BAGNOLO, a town of Venetian-Lombardy, in the deleg. and 8 m. SSW of Brescia. Pop. 3,000.— Also a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 12 m. NW of Saluzzo. Pop. 2,500.

BAGNOLS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Uzes.—The cant. comprises 17 com., and contained in 1831, 16,007 inhabitants. - The village stands on the r. side of the Ceze, on a rocky site, 14 m. NE of Uzes. It is generally ill-built, but contains a fine square surrounded with piazzas, a communal college, and an hospital. It has manufactories of caddis, cards, hats, serge, &c., silk-spinning-mills, dye-works and distilleries of brandy. Its chief articles of commerce are corn, wine, and brandy. Fairs are held three times annually. The locality affords excellent red wines. Pop. 4,902.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Latour. Pop. 1,898
BAGNOLS-LES-BAINS, a large village of France, in the dep. of Lozère, cant. of Blaymard, on the Lot. It has hot sulphureous springs and muchfrequented baths.
BAGNOLS-SUR-MER. See BANYULS-SUR-MER.
BAGNONE, a town of Tuscany, in the deleg. of Pisa, 9 m. SE of Pontremoli, in the Val-di-Magra. Pop. 771.
BAGNOR, a township in the p. of Speen, Berkshire. Area, with Woodspeen, 630 acres. Pop. 700.
BAGNOREA. See BAGNAREA.
BAGNUOLI. See BAGNOLI.

BAGOLINO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 24 m. NNE of Brescia, on the N bank of the Caffaro, at the upper extremity of the Val Sabia. It has iron forges and manufactories of china. In the environs are the hot sulphureous springs of San-Giacomo.
BAGORODSK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 30 m. E of Moscow, on the r. bank of the Kliazma.
BAGOS. See BAGGAS.
BAGRA, a fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Lahore, and dist. of Mundi, situated on a peak 6,168 ft. in height, in N lat. 31° 25'.
BAGRANITZA. See BROUNITZA.
BAGRAS, or BEGRAS, a town of Syria, in the 569. pash. of Aleppo, on the E side of the Almadagh range, 13 m. NNE of Antakia.
BAGRES, or Congos, a river of Spanish Guayana, in the dist. of Maracaibo, which runs in an E direction and falls into Lake Maracaibo at the lagune of Atoles.
BAGRODE, or BURRA, a town and small fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, near Ratghur. It consisted in 1820 of 600 houses. Pop. 1,200.
BAGRU, a river of Guinea, in the Bolon territory, which falls into Sherboro river in N lat. 7° 35'. It is supposed to be an arm of the Camaranca.

BAGSHOT, a chapelry and village in the p. of Windlesham, Surrey, 10 m. SW of Staines, 6 m. from the Woking station on the London and Southampton railway. Pop. 1,071. — Also a hamlet in Shalbourn p., Wiltshire. Pop. 194.
BAGTHORPE, a parish in Norfolkshire, 7 m. SW of Burnham. Area 710 acres. Pop. 78.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Basford, Nottinghamshire.
BAGUA CHICA, a town of Ecuador, in the prov. of Assuay, built on both sides of the Huahua or Utcubambu, 20 m. above its confluence with the Maranon, and 25 m. ESE of Jaen-de-Bracamores.
BAGUAN, a small island in the Asiatic archipelago, near the NE coast of Borneo, in N lat 6°, E long. 118° 35'.
BAGUDRA, a village of Sinde, 24 m. SW of Subzulcote, 10 m. from the l. bank of the Indus.
BAGUER-MORVAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Dol. Pop. 1,979.
BAGUER-PICAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Dol. Pop. 1,654.
BAGULEY, a township in the p. of Bowden, in Cheshire. Area 2,070 acres. Pop. 505.

BAGULCOT, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bejapur, extending on the NE to the river Kistnah, and on the SE to the Malpurba. It is of irregular outline; 54 m. in extreme length, and 44 m. in extreme breadth; but its total superficies does not exceed 1,230 sq. m. It is not intersected by any perennial streams; and the supplies of water from the periodical rains being insufficient for irrigation, the cultivation of the soil is to a considerable extent limited to a strip along the great frontier-rivers, on which also the greater number of the villages are situated. The heat of the dry season is intense. The pergunnah of Bagulcot formed a portion of the territories of the Shah Nur Nabod, which in 1755 fell into the hands of the Maharattas. In 1818 it was added to the British dominions. The pop. is estimated at 100,000. The language is Canarese.
BAGULCOT, the cap. of the above district, contained | lantic, and covered with a light sandy soil on a cal- BAH in 1820, 1,376 houses, with a pop. of 7,523; and possesses an active commerce.

BAGUR, a district of Hindostan, in the provinces of Malwa and Gujerat, extending from Mewar on the N, to Malwa on the S. Its surface, especially towards its W and S confines, is bristled with hills covered with jungle of black-wood, teak, &c., and affording granite, gneiss, and a coarse species of marble. The want of permanent streams renders the soil to a great extent unproductive, and the climate is generally unhealthy. The richest portion of the territory is to the N of Banswara; here vegetation is found of the greatest luxuriance. The principal towns are Dungurpur, Banswara, and Sangwara. The existence of considerable ruins in some localities indicates that the district was formerly one of greater prosperity. The pop. consists of Bheels and Meenas.
BAGWAN. See BAGHWAN.
BAGWORTH, a chapelry in the p. of Thornton, Leicestershire, 4 m. NE of Market-Bosworth. Pop.
BAHADURPUR, a town of Hindostan, in Gujerat, 62 m. E by S of Cambay.—Also a town in Gujerat, 7 m. from Dubboi.—Also a town in Malwa, in the district of Chendari.

BAHA'MAS, or Luca'yos, an extensive chain of low islands, stretching in a SE and NW direction from off the N side of St. Domingo to the coast of East Florida, between 20° and 28° N lat., and 68° and 80° W long. These islands have never been regularly surveyed, nor has their number been ascertained with any certainty; but they have been estimated at 500, of which, however, a great proportion are mere islets and rocks, here called cays or keys; not more than 12 or 14 are inhabited. Their collective area is estimated at 5,424 sq. m. The following are the most worthy of attention. Those marked in italics are inhabited. Harbour island, Abaco or Lucayo, Heneaguas, Acklin's island, Little island, Andrew's island, Long island, Atwood's key, Great Bahama, Long key, New Providence, Berry island, Ragged island, Beminis, Caicos or Caycos, Rose island, Castle island, Royal island, Crooked island, Rum key, Russel island, Eleuthera, St. Salvador or Cat island, Exuma, French keys, Turk's island, Watling island. Hog island, The principal islands are situate on those remarkable flats called the Great and Little banks of Bahama, which are divided by a fathomless strait called Providence channel. The Little B. bank lies between 25° 50' and 27° 30' N lat. The Great Bahama is 63 m. long, and 9 m. broad. It is on the S side of the Little Bahama bank. The passage between this island and the continent, called the channel of Bahama, is 135 m. long, and 46 m. broad. The Gulf-stream runs in this channel at the rate of 5 to 6 m. an hour; and many vessels have been wrecked in passing through this dangerous strait. The Great Bahama sand-bank extends from near Cuba, in 22° 20', to the Bahamas, in 25° 15' N lat. It is about 325 m. in length, and 80 m. in breadth. The deepest water on it is only 30 ft.; while its banks rise almost perpendicularly from an unfathomable depth of water. Between 23° and 24° N lat. lies the Key Sal bank, which turns the Gulf-stream N to the straits of Florida. The Bahama islands have never been correctly delineated in any of the charts of the West Indies. Many of them are situated upon the Great Bahama bank, others upon the Little Bahama bank, and others out of soundings in the Atlantic ocean. They are generally long, narrow, and low, curved towards the Atcareous strata. Though destitute of running streams, they abound in springs. Climate.] The climate is salubrious. In the salt islands, no epidemic disease has ever been known. The more northern islands, during the winter-months, are rendered cool and agreeable by the NW breeze from the continent of America. At New Providence, the therm, in the shade varies from about 85° or 90° in summer, to 60° or 65° in winter. The more southern islands are hotter throughout the year, but they enjoy the cooling sea-breezes which blow within the tropics, though these do not extend to the most northern of the Bahamas. The NE trade-wind prevails throughout the year; except from November to March, when strong gales from the NW are prevalent. The subjoined table exhibits an average of meteorological observations for seven years, taken daily at 9 A. M., 3 P. M., and 9 P. M.; the maximum heat during day, and minimum at night, taken by register-thermometers. The instruments were kept for two years in a wooden house in a room having a NW aspect, and for five years in a substantial stone house in a room having an E aspect; the first about 70 ft. from the sea-side, and 20 ft. above its level, the latter about one-sixth of a mile from the sea, and 90 ft. above its level. The barometer in this climate is scarcely at all affected by damp or dry weather; but much so by wind. In summer, the wind blowing almost always from NE to SE, it varies but little, being highest when the wind is NE, and falling as it verges to SE. It falls also about the time of the heavy rains in spring and autumn. and falls lowest with the W wind, and in proportion for intermediate changes. Bar. Therm. Wind. Inches rain. Productions.] The chief article cultivated in these islands is cotton, though for some time past the produce of this article has very considerably fallen off. In New Providence, and in the out islands of St. Salvador, Long island, Watling's island, Grand Bahama, Beminis, and others, there are thousands of acres of uncultivated land well-adapted to cotton-culture. In the East Indies, and many other fertile soils, the cotton-plant grows too rank, and this over-luxuriance produces wood instead of cotton. Of all the tropical climates in her Majesty's dominions, there is none, perhaps, more happily situated than the B. for the cultivation of cotton; but there is wanted to insure its success capital to expend and judgment to direct the culture. It may be added, that at New Providence, African agricultural labourers abound, willing to be employed as labourers, males at the rate of a quarter of a dollar per day, and women at half that amount; and that Crown land may be purchased at 6s. sterling per acre, and private land at moderate rates. The application of British capital only is wanted to supersede here the native 'bush' by the graceful cotton-plant, with its golden blossoms and silver fleece. [Nassau Guardian.] The cultivation of the pine is increasing; Indian corn is also more grown; and it has been proposed to introduce the orange from St. Michael's, and the grape-fruit, and the shaddock. Sugar-planters have as yet experienced little success; and the profits arising from coffee have been trifling. Guinea and Indian corn, yams, sweet potatoes, plantains, cassava, Indian and pigeon pease, grow in great abundance, besides the generality of tropical fruits, and a great variety of esculent vegetables. Cattle and sheep thrive in most of the islands; and the shores and creeks of all the islands abound in turtle and in excellent fish of various species. The feathered tribe, in all the variety of tropical plumage, is numerous. Several of the islands are well-stocked with timber and dye-woods. Mahogany, Brazilleto, fustic, lignum vitæ, Spanish oak, tamarind, lara wood, iron wood, wild cinnamon, pimento, satin wood, pines, cedars, and many other kinds of wood adapted for ship-building, as well as for the purposes of the mechanic and cabinet-maker, are produced upon these islands. Commerce.] The exports consist of cotton, dyewoods, mahogany, turtle, fruits, and coffee. In 1812, the produce of cotton raised in the B. amounted to 1,348,828 lbs. valued at £50,581; but in 1836 the total export of cotton was only 55,663 lbs. The estimated value of miscellaneous articles, including cattle, fruits, &c., raised in 1812, was £219,225. Value of exports to the United Kingdom, £73,576; to all other parts, £27,230. Value of imports from the United Kingdom, £117,395; from all other parts, £38,662. The circulating currency is estimated at £20,000. The paper currency has been cancelled. The following were the chief statistics of the B. in 1831 and 1845: Pop., whites and free coloured, 3,368 males, 13,032 males. 3,863 females, 13,459 females. Ditto, . . 4,727 males, 181 aliens. Slaves, tons; in 1845, 5,640 tons. In 1803 the total pop. been landed at various times from slavers captured by British vessels cruizing in the vicinity of Cuba. The white Creoles are for the most part settled in New Providence, Harbour island, Eleuthera, and Abaco. They are disinclined to agricultural labour, and chiefly maintain themselves by ship-building, wrecking, turtling, and fishing. The Africans are chiefly employed as labourers, and in salt-raking. The islands are divided into ten parishes. There are 41 places of worship, capable of containing 4,890 souls; and 7 schools at which 2,059 children of both sexes were taught in 1845. History.] The B. island of St. Salvador, then called by the natives Guanahani, was the first land discovered by Columbus in his adventurous voyage in search of a New world, in 1492. About the year 1629, a settlement was established by the British in New Providence, at that time quite uninhabited, the mild and peace able natives having been transported to Hispaniola to work the mines. In 1641, the Spaniards displaced these English settlers, but did not occupy the island themselves. In 1666, another attempt was made to establish a settlement upon it by the British, who soon after abandoned it; but in 1690, the proprietors of Carolina sent thither a small colony. In 1703, a combined French and Spanish force wasted and depopulated New Providence, which afterwards became a haunt of buccaneers and freebooters. In 1718, the British government having resolved to establish a regular colonial administration in the B. islands, sent out a governor to New Providence, to whom the pirates submitted, accepting at the same time the king's pardon. The Americans took possession of New Providence in 1776; and in 1782, that island, with all the other islands of which it was the seat-of-government, surrendered to the Spaniards. In the following year they were again restored to Britain by a Colonel Devanx, an American loyalist, who collected a small body of irregular volunteers, and with this force compelled the Spaniards to surrender the capital. At the termination of the American war, encouragement was held out to the loyalists to settle in the Bahama islands, and Nassau was declared a free port in 1787. There are three other regular ports of entry, viz., Exuma, Caycos, and Turk's island. Nassau is the seat of government, which is similar to that of most of the other British West India islands. There is a governor and council appointed by the Crown, and a house-of-Assembly. Besides the courts of chancery and errors, there are the supreme court, an inferior court-of-common-pleas, and a vice-admiralty court. A chamber of commerce has also been established at Nassau. A militia force is embodied in New Providence and at the Turk's islands.

BAHAR, or BIHAR, [from Vihar, signifying in Sanscrit 'a Buddhish monastery,'] a province of Hindostan, in the presidency of Bengal; bounded on the N by Nepal; on the E by the prov. of Bengal; on the S by Orissa and the Gundwana territory; and on the W by Gundwana, Allahabad, and Oude. It extends between 22° and 27° N lat., and 83° and 87° E long.; and comprises a total superficies of 53,000 sq. m. This prov., the second greatest and one of the most fertile and populous in British India, is divided by the Ganges, by which it is traversed in a WSW direction, into two nearly equal parts. - The northern division, extending to the forests of Nepal and Moreng, is separated, by the Cosi on the E, from the district of Purneah; and on the W from Goruckpur in Oude by the Gunduk and a line bending thence to the Dewah or Gogra. This district, averaging 70 m. in breadth, presents a uniformly level surface, and is watered by numerous streams tributary to the Ganges. It was divided by the emperor Acbar into 4 circars or districts, -Bettiah or Chumparun, Tirhut, Hajipur, and Sarun, with 4 pergunnahs from Monger. - The central division consists of two tracts separated from each other by the Vind'hya hills, and distinguished by general diversity of physical aspect. The first, stretching along the southern bank of the Ganges, from the Caramnassa, by which it is separated from Allahabad, to the ramification of the Vind'hya range which terminates in the Telliyag-hari pass, on the confines of Rajamahal, and averaging 60 m. in breadth, is the most fertile and populous portion of the prov., yielding nearly two-thirds of its total produce. It contains the districts of Bahar, Monger or Monghir, Shahabad, and Rhotas. The central area of this division—the most remarkable for its luxuriance—is generally level, presenting, excepting on its southern and eastern confines, no elevations exceeding the height of 700 ft. It is to the extent of about one-half comprised in the district of Bahar. Rhotas, the most south-westerly of the districts, situated in greater part between the Sone and Caramnassa, is entirely hilly. The second and most southerly of the subdivisions—sometimes called Chuta Nagpurconsists of mountains remarkable for their rugged sterility, and is to a great extent altogether incapable of cultivation. It contains the three modern districts of Palamou, Ramghur, and Kokerah or Chuta Nagpur, so called from its supposed wealth in diamond mines.—The climate of Bahar, as compared with that of Bengal, is temperate. In the level districts, during the hot season, a parching W wind frequently prevails during the day; but it is generally succeeded by a cool breeze from the opposite direction during the night, and continuous gales of considerable duration are not infrequent. In the cold season the range of the thermometer in the hilly districts at sun-rise is from 35° to 40°; and even at Bahar, frost is occasionally experienced. The staple produce of the province is opium, which is still held by the East India company in strict monopoly. The manufacture of nitre has been chiefly confined to the circars of Hagipur and Sarun, but is now BAH being considerably extended. Grain, sugar, oil, indigo, pawn or betel, are cultivated in great abundance, and in smaller quantities cotton, tobacco, &c. Cotton-fabrics are manufactured in all parts of the prov. The preparation of the atar of roses, and of other essential oils, forms an important branch of native industry.
