The relative position of the prov., and the facilities of transit afforded by the numerous and important rivers by which it is intersected, rendered it at an early period the great commercial thoroughfare of the eastern and upper districts of Hindostan; and the prosperity thus attained it has continued to possess throughout all the vicissitudes of its political history.—This prov. is subdivided into the districts of Bhagelpur, Bahar, Tirhut, Saren, and Ramghur.

BAHA'R, a zillah or district in the above prov.; bounded on the N by the Ganges; on the E by Boglipur; on the S by Ramghur; and on the W by Shahabad. Its length from E to W is about 120 m.; its greatest breadth from N to S 80 m. Its superficial area is estimated at 5,358 sq. m. The number of villages which it contains was, according to the latest returns, 6,312; and the pop. 1,340,610. About one-fourth of the pop. are Mahommedans. The great majority of the other three-fourths are Hindus, of whom upwards of 80,000 families are of the Brahmin caste. A large proportion of the surface of this district consists of level, rich, well-cultivated land. Towards the centre a few hills are found, the highest of which may attain an alt. of 700 ft., and towards the S boundary low offshoots of the Vindhyan mountains appear, which stretch themselves, in a succession of hills and narrow valleys, to Cape Comorin. The Ganges in its progress along this district is about a mile in breadth; and during the rainy season lays a considerable extent of country along its banks under water. The Sone or Golden river, joins the Ganges 3 m. below the town of Moneah; the other principal rivers are the Punpun, the Panchane, and the Phalgu or Fulgu. The productions of the soil are wheat, barley, rice, cotton, indigo, and tobacco. The remains of antiquity are exceedingly numerous in this zillah .- The principal towns are Patna, in N lat. 25° 37', the cap. of the prov., Gaya, now the cap. of the zillah, in N lat. 24° 49', and Dinapur, 35 m. S of Patna.—The town of Bahar, in N lat. 25° 13′, 35 m. SE of Patna, is now a place of little importance, and "in a great degree deserted," though once the metropolis of the Magadha kingdom, and the chief seat of Buddhism. It was sacked by the Mahrattas, and the famine of 1770 completed its ruin.
BAHAR. See BAHR.
BAHARI. See BAHIREH.
BAHARI, a town of Upper Egypt, in the district of Thebes, near the r. bank of the Nile, 4 m. SE of Edfu.
BAHAS, a town of Arabia, in the Tehama, near the coast of the Red sea, 27 m. NE of Loheia.
BAHAWULPUR. See BHAWULPUR.

BAHI'A, a maritime province of Brazil, comprehending nearly all the ancient capitania of B., with that of Ilheos. It lies between 11° 30' and 18° 30' S lat. The Rio Real separates it on the N from the prov. of Sergipe, long considered a district of the capitania of Bahia; on the E it is washed throughout 140 leagues of coast by the Atlantic; on the S the Rio Belmonte separated it from the prov. of Porto Seguro, until of late years when, the prov. of Porto Seguro having been abolished, its boundary on the S was extended to the Rio Mucuri, which now separates it from the prov. of Espiritu Sancto. On the W its boundary with the prov. of Minas Geraes runs from the head of the Mucuri N to the Rio Belmonte or Jequitinhonha, which it strikes at the Salto Grande, in the Cordillera dos Aimores; it then runs NW till it strikes the Rio Verde, which from this point to its junction with the San Francisco, forms the remaining part of the boundary-line with Minas Geraes on the E side of the San Francisco. On the W side of that river, the boundary is formed by the Carinheuha, the comarca of Rio San Francisco, formerly belonging to Pernambuco, having been added to B. in 1820. The enlarged W frontier of this prov., coterminous with Goyaz, is separated from the latter by a succession of lofty sierras, from which it crosses to the Rio San Francisco under the 10th parallel; and that river then separates it on the N from Pernambuco.—This prov. is at present divided into thirteen districts: viz., Bahia, Barro do Rio Grande, Cachoeira, Caravellas, Ilheos, Itapicuru, Jacobina, Nazareth, Porto Seguro, Rio de Contas, Santa Fe, Santo Amaro, and Valença. Its superficial area has of course varied with the frequent rearrangement of the administrative divisions of the Brazilian empire; and accordingly various estimates of its area have been formed by different writers. Probably within the enlarged limits we have above assigned to it, the area of this prov. may amount to 120,000 sq. m. Its pop. has been recently estimated at from 650,000 [Kidder], to 800,000 [Weimar Almanach, 1844]. The great Cordillera dos Aimores, traverses the central portion of this prov. from S to N; and is subdivided into the sierras of Cayru, Cupiaba, Itaraca, Mangabeira, Pedra Branca, and Tromba. The watershed of this range lies in general parallel to the coast, at the average distance of 150 m., and about 50 m. from the Rio Francisco. The highest summits of the range are in its southern part, but do not exceed 4,000 ft.—The principal rivers are the Jequitinhonha or Belmonte, the Rio de Contas, the Patipe, the Itapicuru, the Paraguaçu, the Carinhenha, and the San Francisco. The latter river, before it receives the Carinhenha, and enters the prov. of B., has had a course of above 500 m. from its source in Minas Geraes; and before it leaves B. on the frontiers of Sergipe, it has had a course of other 400 m. through an elevated valley, unsusceptible of cultivation probably, and occupied only with cattle-plains. "Population, however, is scattered everywhere, and everywhere cattle are bred, which are sometimes full fed in abundant pastures, and at other times endure the extreme of want, according to the season." [Southey.] The Paraguaçu is navigable from Cachoeira to the bay of Bahia or All Saints' bay; and flowing through the best cultivated portion of the whole prov., is the most important stream of B., commercially considered. The largest lake in the prov. is that of Itahipe, which discharges itself by the river of that name. It is only 7 m. in circumf. Several lagunes occur on the coast.—Since 1845 diamonds have been found in quantity in the "Chapada" district, 120 m. W of Cachoeira. Gold and copper are no longer wrought. Rock-salt occurs on the Rio San Francisco, and saltpetre in the sertoens or dry upland interior districts. Maize, tobacco, cotton, sugar, and coffee are extensively cultivated; and the fruits of tropical climes are here rich and abundant. The forests supply various species of cabinet-wood, gums, and medical plants, such as jalap, ipecacuanha, and sassafras. The most fertile portion of the prov. is the Reconcavo, or the district surrounding the cap. and All Saints' bay, and extending from 20 to 30 m. into the interior. The vine bears a triple succession of fruit in the year. A considerable extent of the surface is here appropriated to the cultivation of the sugar-cane. A large manufactory of coarse cotton cloth is carried on at Valença, about 30 m. S of Bahia. The comarca of Bahia formerly extended along the coast from the Rio Real to the Jiquiriça, and from the Beira Mar to the frontiers of the inland comarca of Jacobina. Under recent arrangements it only comprises the city of Bahia, and the towns of Abrantes, Jaguaripe, and Maragogipe. The city of Bahia, or San Salvador, the cap. of the above prov., and, with the exception of Rio, the largest and most flourishing city in Brazil, stands on the E shore or right side of the Bahia de Todos os Sanctos, or bay of All Saints. The land at a small distance from the shore rises steeply to a high ridge, on the summit of which, near 650 ft. above sea-level, the Cidade Alta, or Old city, is erected. The buildings in this part of the city are chiefly of the 17th cent. The houses are of stone, from three to five stories in height, and of a respectable though somewhat decayed appearance. Here are the cathedral, the Jesuits' college-now the ecclesiastical one-and monastery, now converted into a Franciscan convent, several churches, the palace of the governor, the hall of the senate, and the other chief public buildings. The Cidade Baixa, or Praya, is an immense street, nearly 4 m. in length, running along the beach, and containing the trapiches or magazines, and warehouses. At its S extremity are the arsenal and docks; and, instead of as formerly at Itapagipe, the ship-yards, in which merchant-vessels are built. The customhouse and wharfs are on the beach. A steep and difficult ascent leads to the Cidade Alta; and between the upper and the lower towns, some of the streets wind in a devious course along ravines, while others slant across an almost perpendicular bluff. A few of the superior class of inhabitants have large and elegant mansions; and others may be found roomy and convenient, though from the street they have a dull and dirty appearance; but the houses of tradesmen and shopkeepers are often disgustingly dirty, and instead of glazed windows have only wooden lattices; while the lower order of soldiers, Mulattoes, and Negroes, occupy tiled cabins without a ceiling, and with a single latticed window; and buildings of this wretched description are scattered all over the city. The public promenade has been formed on the boldest and most commanding height of the upper town. One of its sides opens towards the ocean; and another up the bay. The variety and beauty of the trees and flowers, and the sweep of the magnificent bay beneath, render this a delicious promenade. The hedges of the suburbs of B. are composed of lime-trees, the leaves of which emit a delicious fragrance; and large jaca-trees, with their heavy fruit clinging to the limbs and the trunk, are numerous. The English cemetery on the Victoria hill is finely situated; and the whole environs of B. are very beautiful, and being constantly refreshed by alternate land and sea-breezes-though the temp. all the year round is from 75° to 85°—are deemed healthy and salubrious. "Few cities," Mr. Kidder says, "present a single view of more imposing beauty than does B., to a person beholding it from a suitable distance on the water. Even Rio de Janeiro can hardly be cited for such a comparison. That city excels in the endless variety of its beautiful suburbs; yet I should be at a loss to point out one which, in all respects, equals that part of B. known as the Victoria hill. In Rio, one section competes with another, and each offers some ground of preference; but in B., the superiorities seem all to be united in one section, leaving the foreigner no room for doubt or discussion respecting the best quarter for locating his residence. On the Victoria hill may be found the finest gardens that B. afford, the most enchanting walks, and the most ample shade. Here too are the best houses, the best air, the best water, and the best society. The walls of two ancient and extensive forts, also ado very much to the romantic and historical interest of the place. In fine, he who looks for any one spot that combines more of external beauty, than does that to which I refer, will roam long and widely over the face of the earth." In 1803, the pop. of this city was estimated at 100,000, of whom 30,000 were whites, 30,000 Mulattoes, and 40,000 Negroes. Mr. Kidder-who though severe on the inaccuracies of others, has himself really contributed little to our knowledge of Brazilian statistics—is silent as to the pop. even of this important city, though resident in it for some time. Red pottery is manufactured at on the W side of the island, called the Barra Falsa, (f), but it is not above 2 m. wide, and so shallow as to admit only coasting-vessels. The bay itself is an WWW m extensive gulf, upwards of 30 leagues in circumf. It is 23 m. long from N to S, and 30 m. wide from E to W [Henderson], and presents a safe anlat. 13° 0′ 44", W long. 38° 31′ 28" [Roussin], W long. 38° 33½' [Sabine]. Several batteries line the E side of the entrance; but the principal defence is Fort do Mar, or San Marcello (m), built on a small parica is 20 m. long, and 5 m. in average breadth, and has a pop. of 16,000. It is covered with villages, and groves of cocoa - palms, mangoes, jacas, and oranges. Above 100 little islets are sprinkled over the surface of this noble bay. The commerce of this port, as well as of the prov. of B, has recently, and chiefly by the great value of diamonds exported, been on the increase, having been declining since 1837. The planters of coffee in the S of the prov., send their crops to the market of Rio de Janeiro, where they obtain better prices. The crops of tobacco have greatly diminished. In 1817, the quantity exported was 660,000 arrobas of 32 lbs. each; and in 1840, only 231,243 arrobas; snowing a decrease of 428,757 arrobas. The export of cotton has declined very considerably, and it now constitutes an insignificant item. On the other hand, the sugar crop in 1847 was 50,000, and in 1850 about 60,000 the empire. tons, nearly twice that of 20 years back. The export also of many articles, as tapioca, piassava, timber, cocoa, and especially of diamonds-valued in 1849 at £170,000 sterling—has sprung up within the above period. Fishers, . . *** The number of British vessels which arrived at B. in 1844 was 99, manned by 1,252 men, and of a total tonnage of 22,886 tons; the total foreign tonnage entering the port in that year being 70,111 tons. In 1845, the British tonnage entering B. was 26,874; the total foreign tonnage being 89,347. The Swedish and Norwegian tonnage ranks next to that of Britain in the commerce of this port. The principal import made at this port still continues to be that of slaves. The force of popular opinion, or interest rather, and the weakness of the government, combine to render futile all attempts to prevent the landing and sale of slaves here; and the number annually imported is said to range from 12,000 to 20,000 .- B. was founded about 1549, and was, until 1763, the cap. of Brazil. BAHIA BLANCO, a settlement on the coast of Buenos Ayres, about 120 m. to the N of the town of Carmen.
BAHIA DE CHETAMEL, or HANOVER BAY, a bay on the E coast of Yucatan, in N lat. 18° 45', W long. 89° 15'.
BAHIA ESCOCESA, or Scotch Bay, a bay on the N coast of Hayti, to the W of Cape Cabron.
BAHIA FERMOSA, or FAIR BAY, a bay on the E coast of Brazil, in S lat. 6° 12', about 6 m. in extent from N to S.
BAHIA HONDA, a large bay on the coast of Colombia, the E point of which is 4 m. from Point Aguja,—the Bahia

HONDA CHICA, or Little Bay Honda, lying between them. The entrance to the larger bay is 3 m. wide, and the bay itself is shallow and clean. From the W point of this bay, the coast runs SW 11 m. to the bay of El Portete.-Also a spacious and well-sheltered harbour on the NW coast of the island of Cuba, having its entrance in S lat. 22° 58' 59", E long. 83° 6', between El Morillo on the E, and Punta Pescadores on the W. A shoal called the Bahia bank lies 54 leagues NW by N from its mouth. -Also a bay on the coast of Florida, between Love kay and Cow kays, in N lat. 24° 33', W long. 81° 26'.
BAHIA LONGA, a bay on the coast of Benguela, between Point Longo and Point Morro, which lie 13 leagues N and S of each other.
BAHIA NEGRO, a lake of Bolivia, 5 leagues long from N to S, formed by the surplus waters from the Albuquerque plains, and discharging itself into the Paraguay, 30 m. S of Coimbra.—Mawe.
BAHIA DE NUESTRA SENORA, a bay on the Atacama coast of Bolivia, in S lat. 25° 30', W long. 70° 40'.
BAHIA NUEVA, or New Bay, a bay on the E coast of Patagonia, on the SW side of the peninsula on which Port Valdez stands. Its S point, Punta Niufas, is in S lat. 42° 58', W long. 64° 20′.
BAHIA TORTA, a fine bay on the coast of Benguela, near Point Salinas.
BAHIA TRAIZAO, or TREASON BAY, a bay on the E coast of Brazil, having its N point in S lat. 6° 41' 15", W long. 34° 57' 23". To the N of this point, the coast becomes dry, sterile, and sandy.

BAHIA DE TUCACAS, or Golfo Trieste, an extensive bay on the coast of Venezuela, whose N point, Punta Tucacas, bears NW by N, 25 m. from the entrance of Puerto Cabello. The shore is low and covered with mangroves; the interior rises into thickly wooded mountains. There are several islets in the bay. BAHIOUDA, BAYUDAH, or BEHEDA, a desert tract of Nubia, to the S of Dongola, inhabited by the Kababish Arabs.

BAHIREH, or BAHAYREH, a prov. of Lower Egypt, on the W side of the Canopic branch of the Nile, divided into the departments of Neghiteh and Damanhur. The name was formerly applied to the whole Delta. The canal of B., 104,000 yards in length, united the Rosetta branch of the Nile with Lake Mareotis.
BA'HLINGEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Rhine, 10 m. NW of Friburg. Pop. 1,750.

BAHLINGEN, or Balingen, a bailiwick in Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black-forest, intersected by the Eisach river, and containing 2 towns, and 21 villages.—The cap. of the same name lies on the Eisach, to the NE of Rothweil. Pop. 3,250. A considerable quantity of linen and woollen cloth is manufactured here.
BAHN, a village of Prussia, in Pomerania, 12 m. SSE of Stettin, on the Tuhe, an affluent of the Oder. Pop. 1,871. Straw-hats are extensively manufactured here.
BAHNACEH, or Bahnasa', a village of Central Egypt, in N lat. 38° 33', near the Bahr Yusuf, due W of Abugirge. It is on the site of the ancient Oxyrynchus.
BAHNASA. See Oasis Parva.

BAHR, the Arabic term for a sea, lake, river, or any large body of water, and hence of frequent occurrence in the composition of names in Oriental geography. Its diminutive is Boheirah, 'a small lake.'
BAHR-EL-ABIAD. See ABIAD.

BAHR-ABUL-MUNEJJA, a canal branching off from the E side of the Nile, 6 m. below Cairo, and running past Balbeis into the Wadi Tumilat, where it probably joined the canal uniting the Red sea with the Nile.
BAHR-EL-ADA, a river of Africa, rising in the Shilluk country, and flowing into the Bahr-el-Abiad, near Jebel Abid, in N lat. 11°.
BAHR-ASAL. See ASAL.
BAHR-EL-AZREK. See ABAI. BAH BAHR-BILA'-MA', [i. e. 'the Waterless sea,'] or

BAHR-EL-FARGH, a valley in the Libyan desert, divided by a sandy ridge on the E from the valley of the Natron lakes. It is about 9 m. in breadth; and according to Andreossi extends above 30 m. in a NNW and SSE direction.—There is another Bahrbila-Ma between the Bahr Yusuf and Lake Mœris. It appears to have been an artificial outlet by which the superabundant waters of the Bahr Yusuf were carried into the lake.

BAHR-EL-BACAREH, a village on the Nile, corresponding with the ancient Cercasorum, at a point where the river divides into two branches, one of which runs NW and falls into the sea below Rosetta, the other runs due N passing Damietta.
BAHR-BATN, or BAHR-FAYYA'D, a large canal, or parallel branch of the Nile.
BAHR-EL-GHAZAL, a fertile valley in Sudan, lying between Lakes Tchad and Fittre, and inhabited by Tibbús. BAHR-EL-HOULE, [i. e. 'Lake of Julias,'] or

BAHR KLAIT, a sheet of water in Syria, near the sources of the Jordan, the ancient lake of Samachonitis. It is about 7 m. long, and 2 m. broad; but its waters are muddy and unwholesome: in fact, after the snows are melted and the waters fallen, this lake is only a marsh through which the Jordan runs. Its shores abound in wild boars which find concealment in the reeds and rushes. Sedges, rushes, and lotus plants cover a great part of its surface. Richardson says that this lake is formed by the confluence of innumerable mountain-streams and springs rising from the mountains all round, at a greater or shorter distance, and formed by the rain and the melting of the snows, each contributing its portion to the formation of the Jordan, and that no two of them surpass the others so much as to entitle them to the credit of forming this lake. [Travels, vol. ii.] The greatest body of the lake is to the W of the point of efflux of the Jordan.

BAHR-EL-MERJ, or Bahairat-el-Merj, [i. e. 'Lake of the Meadows,'] a lake or swamp of Syria, in the pashalik of Damascus, into which the Lowa, the Barrada, and other streams descending from the eastern declivity of the Antilibanus discharge themselves. Haji Khalifah says that it abounds in fish, and contains a forest of bulrushes; Volney describes it as a mere marsh; Seetzen delineates it as a lake. It is probably of varying dimensions and depth.

BAHR-NEGASH, or Bar-Nagashi, a district of Abyssinia, being the first terrace or upland of Tigre, in advancing from Arkiko, on the Red sea, northwards into the interior. It comprises the districts of Hamazen, Kok, Falasha, Serawe, Dixan, Halai, and Sewan.

BAHR-EL-SAFFI, a district of Hadramaut, in Arabia, a part of the desert El Akhaf. Baron Wrede describes it as an immense sandy plain, strewn with numberless undulating hills of sand, which give it the appearance of a moving sea, and without a single speck of vegetation. It contains quicksands, one of which, he says, he attempted to sound with a plummet and a cord of 60 fath. which he threw into it; but in five minutes the last trace of the cord disappeared. — Geog. Journ. vol. xiv. p. 110.

BAHR-TOUMAT. See TOUMAT. BAHR YUSUF, [i. e. 'Joseph's river,'] an ancient canal, or rather arm of the Nile, running parallel with the river, along the foot of the hills between Assiout and Fayum. It has a mean breadth of 328 ft. This canal has stopt the further encroachment of the Desert on this side, and secured the irrigation of the land between itself and the Nile. At Medmetel-Fayum, it branches off into a number of small streams.

BAHRAHAH, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, between Aidun and Tugbul. BAHREIN, [i. e. 'the Two seas,'] a district of Arabia, stretching along a gulf of the same name, on the W shore of the Persian gulf, between Ras Reccan, in N lat. 26° 12', E long. 51° 13', and Ras Tannora or El Katif, in N lat. 26° 28', E long. 50° 5'. [Buckingham.] Its shores are low and sandy to the E of B. island, until near Cape Mussendum, and the only vegetation they exhibit consists of a few shrubs, mostly a species of balsam. To the W it attains a moderate height in the vicinity of El Katif. In the bottom of the bay is a capacious creek, extending several miles inland, called Laghere, in which many of the boats attending the pearl-fishery are laid up during the winter.

BAHREIN ISLANDS, a cluster of islands on the SW side of the Persian gulf, in the middle of Bahrein bay, the principal of which are named Bahrein or Aval, and Arad. The first,-which is considered the chief, - lies about 10 m. from the Arabian coast, and 90 m. WNW of Bushire. It is 27 m. in length from N to S, and 10 m. in breadth according to some; but only 10 m. in length, and about 5 m. in breadth according to Buckingham. It is covered with villages and date-gardens, and produces figs, citrons, peaches, and almonds. Its shores are low; but a range of hills intersects its centre. Its chief town, called Manamora, at the NE extremity, in N lat. 26° 14', is large and populous. The tide here rises 7 ft. The town is destitute of water, the supplies of this necessary article being brought from the interior. [Tanner.] It has a harbour wellsheltered from all points except the NW, with from 3 to 41 fath. water on a sandy bottom, admitting vessels of 200 tons, which, with a fair wind, are carried from this to Bushire in 14 hours. [Kinneir.] The island of Arad lies on the N of Bahrein, forming the E side of Bahrein harbour. Buckingham says it is nearly of the same length as the larger island, but at its greatest width does not exceed 2 m. The direction of its length is nearly N and S, and its centre in N lat. 26° 15', E long. 50° 40'. On its W and N sides springs of fresh water gush from submarine rocks, some of them in 3 fath. water. There are also springs of fresh water under the sea near the NE part of B. island. At its SW extremity is the town of Maharag. A pearl-fishery,—the richest and most productive in the world,—is conducted on an extensive scale at these islands. The oyster banks, lying 15 or 20 ft. below the surface, stretch from about 25° to 26° 40' N lat. Two kinds are obtained here,—the yellow pearl, which is disposed of among the Mahrattas,—and the white, which is carried through Bassora and Bagdad, into Asia; considerable quantities are sent to Constantinople; and the remainder reaches Europe. The pearls of B. are said to be more durable than those of Ceylon, and the Indians prefer them on account of their permanence of colour. The shells-some of which are from 5 to 10 in. in diam., and thick in proportion— are valuable as furnishing excellent mother-of-pearl. The fishing, which continues about two months, employs 2,400 boats, each manned by from 8 to 20 men. The divers are Negroes and Arab slaves. About the year 1790, this fishery was farmed-out by the different chiefs along the coast; it was next carried on by individuals, and the profits amounted to about £100,000 annually. Though the banks have been equally productive since, yet the value of the produce has declined, in consequence of the transference of the English market to the straits of Manaar. The pearls are now chiefly sent to Surat.—The B. islands once belonged to the Portuguese; but they were seized by an Arabian chief, who in his turn became tri- * BAI butary to the Persians. They now pay a tribute of 30,000 dollars yearly to the imam of Oman.-These islands were known to the ancients under the names of Aradus and Tylos, which were likewise the names of two Phoenician cities.—Buckingham.—Parsons.— Lieut. Tanner in Asiat. Journ., vol. vi., p. 464.
BAHUL, a lofty mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Santa Catharina, near Porto Bello.
BAHUS, a town of Sweden, 18 m. N of Gottenburg, in N lat. 57° 52', E long. 11° 42'.
BAHRENBURG, a village of Hanover, 20 m. SW of Hoya, on the Suhlingen river. Pop. 575.
BAHRI, a town of Hindostan, 10 m. N of the river Chumbul, in E long. 77° 35', N lat. 26° 47'.

BAIA, or BAIE, a small village of Naples, in the Terra di Lavoro, 2 m. W of Pozzuoli, 11 m. W of Naples, on the site of the ancient Baiæ. It is situated in a bay of the gulf of Naples, between Lake Averno and Cape Misenum; and was celebrated in ancient times as one of the most delicious spots in Italy; its port was deep and sheltered from the winds, and the town built close to the sea-shore. The number of wealthy Romans who resided here rendered it rich and flourishing; but the Lombards, and afterwards the Saracens, ruined it so completely that nothing remains of the town but a few small cottages and the pavement of the quay; and the once smiling shore is now dreary and desolate, having been desolated by subterranean fires and earthquakes. [Simond.] It is, however, highly interesting to the antiquary; and is besides famous for its warm baths, on the slope of Monte Grillo. Don Pedro di Toledo, viceroy under Charles V., erected a castle on a hill to defend the entrance of the harbour; and about the beginning of last cent., a small fort was built on an island in the harbour which communicates with the shore by a bridge.

BAIAD, or BAYAD DEIR, a town of Central Egypt, in the prov. of Atfieh, on the r. bank of the Nile, opposite Benisuef. It is inhabited by Christians, who carry on a considerable trade in poultry and eggs. It was the scene of a conflict between Hassein Bey and Ali Bey, towards the end of the last cent.
BAIANICI, or BOIANIEC, a town of Austria, in Galicia, on an affluent of the Reta, 15 m. above the junction of that river with the Bug, and 22 m. N of Lemburg.
BAIAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 110 m. SSW of Para, on the E bank of the Tocantines. Cocoa, cotton, and coffee are cultivated in the environs.
BAIAS. See BAYAS.

BAIBUT, or BAIBURDI, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. and 60 m. WNW of Erzerum, on the r. bank of the Chorak. Pop. 3,000. It is defended by an ancient castle of great strength, which stands on the summit of an isolated hill to the N of the town. The environs afford timber, wax, and honey. The quantity of snow which falls in the vicinity in winter, generally precludes the town for several months from communication with the surrounding country.-B. is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Varutha, founded by a Scythian colony in the time of Alexander, and which attained considerable splendour in the reign of the Seljuk sultans.
BAIDI, a town of Tibet, 70 m. SW of Lassa, at the W foot of Mount Gambala.
BAIDO, a town of Sicily, 5 m. WNW of Castela-Mare.
BAIDU, a river of Tibet, which takes its rise on the N side of Mount Baidu, and running in a N direction, unites with the Murui-ussu in E long. 92° 32'.

BAIDYANATH, or DEOGHUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, and dist. of Birbum, 110 m. WNW of Murshedabad. It is situated on an eminence in the midst of extensive forests; and is 2 L celebrated for its temple, dedicated to the god of medicine, which contains a lingam of great legendary renown. There are also several sacred pools; but a river mentioned in the Puranas has now no existence.

BAIE DES ALLOUETTES. See SAGUENAY. BAIE ST. ANTOINE, or LEFEBVRE, a seignory of Lower Canada, in the co. of Gamaska, extending 6 m. in breadth along the SE bank of the St. Lawrence, to the confluence of the Nicolet, and bounded on the SE by Courland. Its soil is fertile and productive; and the margin of the bays which indent its W border afford pasturage of the richest description. The v. is situated on a rising ground in the centre of the seignory. Total pop. 2,955. BAIE ST. PAUL. See COTE DE BEAUPRE.
BAIERSBRONN, a parish of Wurtemberg, 3 m. NNW of Freudenstadt, in a valley of the same name, near the r. bank of the Murg. In the vicinity are the forges of Friedrichsthal. Pop. 4,359.

BAIERSDORF, or BAYERSDORF, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Rezat, 5 m. NNE of Erlangen, on the r. bank of the Regnitz. It has extensive nailmanufactories. The environs produce tobacco and fruits. Pop. 1,500, of whom 450 are Jews.

BAIGNES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, and arrond. of Barbezieux. The cant. contains 9 com., and possessed, in 1831, a pop. of 7,991. The village is 8 m. SSW of Barbezieux; and formerly contained an abbey, reputed to have been founded by Charlemagne. It has a manufactory of delft-ware, and tanneries, and a considerable commerce in cattle and pigs. Pop. 200.

BAIGNEUX-LES-JUIFS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, and arrond. of Chatillon.—The cant. comprises 16 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 5,490.—The town is finely situated, 18 m. SSE of Chatillon, and 28 m. NW of Dijon. The soil of the environs is stony, and the supply of water inadequate for its culture. Pop. 483.

BAIGORRY, or Baigorri, a valley of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, and arrond. of Mauleon. It is watered by the Nive, and is 12 m. in length, and 9 in breadth. It contains extensive copper-mines. The Basque is the prevailing dialect of the inhabitants.

BAIGOURA, a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, and cant. of Etienne de Baigorry. It rises on the E of the Nive, and is about 5 m. in extent. It consists of argillaceous schist, with thick beds of quartz.
BAIGTS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, and cant. of Mugron. Pop. 1,049.

BAIKAL, an extensive lake in the mountainranges which skirt the table-land of Northern Asia, between the parallels of 51° and 56° N, and between 104° and 111° E long. It is upwards of 350 m. long, and its breadth varies from 16 to 52 m. Its mean breadth may be between 30 and 40 m.; and it exceeds 1,000 m. in circuit. Its surface is estimated at 625 German sq. m., or 13,723 English sq. m.; while the Caspian sea has a superficies of 7,375 German sq. m.; the Aral, 2,100; Lake Superior, 1,700; Lake Tchad, 680? Great Bear lake, 675; Great Winnipeg, 565; and Lake Ladoga, 320. The greater portion of the lake extends in a SW and NE direction. Its depth, though unequal, is in some places incredibly great: in some of the central gulfs a line of more than 300 fath. did not reach the bottom. Of the rivers that discharge themselves into the B., the principal are the Upper Angarà, the Bargusin, and the Selinga, which respectively join it from the N, the E, and the S. The only outlet for the surplus waters of this enormous reservoir is the Lower or Great Angara, western side with impetuous rapidity, through a deep channel more than a mile broad, and interspersed with huge fragments of rock, presents a scene of awful sublimity. It cannot be supposed that this single channel is at all adequate to the discharge of such a prodigious body of water as is conveyed to it by the three great rivers just mentioned, besides nearly 200 minor streams and rivulets; yet the lake seldom rises, even in the spring-season, more than 3 ft. above its ordinary level; so that it appears probable that a part of its waters may be absorbed by some subterraneous drain. "The bed of the lake must be of an unusual nature to receive and absorb the quantities of water which are annually poured into it, and which never make any sensible difference in its height, be they more or less. It never overflows its banks, and its greatest height instead of being in spring on the melting of the snows, is in autumn, when there is a variation of from seven to fourteen inches, but not more. Fogs are constant upon it even in summer, which makes it always cold, and, doubtless, a certain quantity of water is carried off by evaporation, but that is insufficient, comparing it with other similar waters, to account for the level being so regularly kept. In the summer of the year 1818, the water rose suddenly above 6 ft., and remained so for a considerable time without any apparent cause, since which the level of the lake is known to be a little higher than it was before. Contemporaneously with this sudden rising of the lake, a small river, Iréte, distant from it in a straight line 120 versts to the NW, suddenly overflowed its banks. The source of this river is at the foot of a mountain, and it is immediately of a considerable size. Its inundation was subterranean and instantaneous, the waters pouring out in great bodies from the source itself. It is possible that a communication may exist between the two by subterranean channels, some of which had been stopped up, and the water not finding its usual exit, raised the level of the lake, as well as river, from the same reason. In a summer of great drought, an extraordinary rising of the Léna took place some years ago, in the same manner, which destroyed all the hay of the poor people who inhabited its banks. These sudden and unaccountable elevations of the water threaten to be some day fatal to the town of Irkutsk; the passage of the Angara through the narrow valley would, by any increase in the volume of its waters, be necessarily too narrow to contain them." [Cottrell.] The water of the B. is so clear that at the depth of 8 fath. the bottom is distinctly seen; at a distance it assumes a greenish hue. It is agreeable to the taste, except in the month of July, when it is thrown into a kind of fermentation, called its flowering, which renders it somewhat nauseous, and gives it a turbid appearance as if mixed with yellow sand. Of the islands of the B.—which are numerous along the E shore—the most remarkable is the island Olkhon, near the N shore, separated from the main land by a sound. This island is 32 m. in length from SW to NE, and nearly 10 m. in breadth; and is inhabited by about 150 Buriate families. It terminates in a promontory towards the N; on the SE it is low and bare; but its SW coast is finely diversified by groves of poplars, willow, birch, and pines. Here the pasture is so rich that large droves of cattle are maintained upon it through the winter, without any particular care or tending. The coasts of the B. present many objects which well-deserve the attention of the naturalist. Springs impregnated with sulphur and naphtha are to be found in various places. One of these, opposite the W side of Olkhon, is so extremely copious as to flowing N to the Yenisei, which, bursting from its | yield 582 gallons every hour. Its water, being highly sulphureous, has a fœtid taste; and is so hot that birds are boiled in it in the space of 12 minutes. To the S of Bargusin peninsula, there is a lake called Dukhovoi, -i. e. 'Vapoury,'-the water of which is slimy and yellowish, and has a nauseous acid taste. The whole district is affected by its fœtid exhalations, yet the water itself, when taken in a vessel, has no offensive odour. The lake even swarms with various kinds of fish, which are often stifled, however, during winter, when the putrid water is covered by an impenetrable coat of ice. Of all the mineral waters on the coast of the B., none is more celebrated than the Turkobad, which, issuing from 7 springs,-some of them cold and others hot,-flows into one reservoir, and is found very beneficial in many disorders. Near the same spot, is found naphtha which the lake throws out in the spring, in lumps sometimes 2 or 3 inches in diam. It is a dark-brown clammy substance, capable of being kneaded, soluble in water of a moderate heat, and of a pleasant odour. The coasts of the B. likewise abound in alkaline salts of different kinds. The plants most frequent on the sandy coast of the B., and in the neighbouring forests, are such as generally grow on very cold mountains. Those enumerated by Pallas, are the Pinus cembra, the Ems petrum nigrum, the Campanula with round leaves and large flowers, the Fumaria impatiens, the Polygonum divaricatum, the Polygonum sericeum, a superb specieof knotgrass indigenous to the shores of this lake, the Scrophularia scorodonia, the Dracocephalum nutans, the Lycopsis vesicaria, and the Triticum littorale, which grows upon the shores in as great abundance as if it were sown, and is so like a kind of barley that the peasants call it Dikaia Koch, i. e. 'wild barley.' Besides these, Pallas observed in the forests, the Lonicera carulea and pyrenaica, the Linnea, the Rubus articus, the Pedicularis paniculata, the Ledum palustre, the Andromeda polifolia, and various kinds of Pyrolæ, among others the Pyrola uniflora. The growth of these mountain-plants is fostered by the cold and hazy air which prevails during summer on the lake, the high mountains which stretch along the S part of the country, and the snow-clad summits which border the W side of the Baikal. In the lake itself there grows a kind of sponge, sweet and thick, which has never been discovered in any other part of the world. In the language of the country it is called morskaia souba, or 'sea sponge;' Pallas has given it the name of Spongia Baikalensis. It is employed by the goldsmiths of Irkutsk to give the first polish to their silver plate, and to vessels of copper and of brass. The animal productions of the B. are also curious. Of these, the most remarkable is a fish peculiar to this lake, called by the Russians solomiianka, and known to naturalists as the Callyonymos Baikalensis. It resembles a clue of blubber; and when exposed on a gridiron to the most gentle heat, melts so completely away that nothing remains of it but a slender bone. It is impossible to catch these fish in nets, nor indeed are they ever seen alive. They seem to confine themselves to the deep gulfs in the centre of the lake; and are generally thrown up to the surface m summer, during the violent hurricanes which burst from the mountains. When the lake has been strongly agitated, they are forced up in such quantities as to form a kind of parapet upon the shore. They are so rank and oily, that neither sea-fowl nor ravens will touch their carcase; and after remaining an hour or two on shore, are dissolved by a slight pressure in the hand. The oil made of their blubber is sold to the Chinese, who value it highly. Seals, likewise, abound in the B.; a remarkable fact, as these a nimals are never seen elsewhere at any distance from the ocean, nor do they frequent rivers or lakes of fresh water. It appears probable, therefore, that they have been introduced into this inland sea by some extraordinary revolution, which has produced a considerable change in the level of the globe. Their skin is of a silver grey, and their number is so great that about 2,000 are taken annually. The hunting of these animals commences in April. They assemble in great flocks, where the rapid currents or warm springs make chasms in the ice, and frequently come out of the water to bask and sleep in the sunshine. The hunters, acquainted with their haunts, place themselves in small sledges, which they conceal with a screen of white linen, which can be moved towards the seals without alarming them; the hunters can thus approach so near as to fire upon them without the possibility of missing their aim. Another mode of catching these animals was observed by Bell of Antermony. Holes were cut in the ice at certain distances, and nets were extended from one hole to another by means of long poles: the seals, unable to remain long under the ice, come to these apertures for air, and thus entangling themselves in the nets, became an easy prey. But the most important fish in this lake is the omul, which, both from its abundance and its excellent quality, is of inestimable benefit to all the surrounding country. This fish varies in size in different parts of the lake. Those caught near the mouth of the Selenga, rarely exceed two spans in length; while those which frequent the bay of Tschivirkoui, are represented by Pallas as enormous, though he does not state their particular dimensions. They bear a considerable resemblance to the herring; but Gmelin asserts, that the only property which they have in common with that fish is the glittering of their scales. Their flesh is white and tender; and they are so extremely delicate that they die as soon as they are taken out of the water. They are generally caught in the month of October; and, instead of being salted, are left by the fishermen to stiffen with the frost, which enables them to convey them fresh to the various markets, and to sell them at a higher price. Towards the middle of August, the omuls divide into shoals, and ascend the rivers for the purpose of spawning. It is remarkable that they generally resort to the same stream in which they themselves were spawned; there are some rivers that flow into the B., which they never visit, though shoals of them may be seen near their mouths. They advance up the rivers very slowly, halting regularly where the current is least rapid; and when they reach the ice, they are compelled to return. This fish — to which naturalists have given the name of Salmo migratorius—is not confined to the B. They are supposed, indeed, to have come originally from the Frozen ocean, from which they force themselves up the Yenisei, and several other rivers. Besides the species of fish which we have already mentioned, several other varieties are found in the B., such as sturgeon, carp, tench, lampreys or Salmo oxyrrinchus, and lenki or Salmo salvelinus. The fishery of the B. is farmed, and is extremely productive and valuable. It is prosecuted during the whole summer, with large dragnets upwards of 200 fath. long, to which is fastened a strong cord of about 1,500 ft. The net is drawn up by means of a windlass, to which the cord is attached. Pallas describes a net which is likewise employed on the B., and which is precisely the same as the stake-nets used in our salmon fisheries. This lake itself can only be regarded as an enormous gulf formed by the rending of the mountains; its rocky shores in almost every spot, present visible marks of some terrible revolution, of which they indicate at the same time the remote antiquity. Its channel consists of the broken fragments of hills, the largest of which still rise above the surface in the form of islands. Its coast is one heap of broken rocks piled above each other to the height of 40 fath. Cliffs, whose bases are sunk in unfathomable pits, lift their shattered summits to the clouds; and, on the pinnacles of the loftiest mountains, are found enormous stones in whimsical shapes, which could only be placed in their present position by some violent convulsion of the earth. Indeed it appears that some volcanic agency is constantly at work in this region. Earthquakes are still frequent in the surrounding regions, but they are in general so slight that their shock is not felt at any considerable distance. The most remarkable effect of these earthquakes is visible in the lake itself, which, even in the serenest weather, and while its surface is smooth as glass, sometimes undergoes violent internal agitations. At times, too, in a particular part of the lake, a single wave will suddenly rise, which is succeeded by several others in the same spot. Most of the phenomena, indeed, observable in the B., seem to be peculiar and anomalous. The state of its surface is almost entirely independent of the violent storms to which it is subject. Even in a very moderate breeze it often rages with alarming fury, while the strongest gales scarcely produce any perceptible increase of agitation. These peculiarities render the navigation of this lake extremely hazardous; for, however inviting the weather may be, and however propitious the gale, a vessel—especially the flat-bottomed merchant vessels used in the navigation from Irkutsk to Kiakhta and Nerchinsk-may suddenly be wrecked by one of those violent commotions which no sagacity can foresee and no activity control. Furious hurricanes, too, often burst in a moment from the surrounding mountains; and if, on these occasions, the bark happens to be in a narrow or shallow part of the lake, its destruction is inevitable. At a distance from the shore, the danger is less imminent; as in the middle of the lake there are no hidden rocks nor banks against which a vessel can strike. The mariners who navigate the B. have a compass peculiar to themselves, in which they distinguish only three winds. Those which blow between the N and S are called Bargusin, because they proceed from the direction of the river Bargusin: when they prevail, the passage is expeditious from the mouth of the Selinga to the opposite shore. Those which arise between the NW and SW are called Kultuk, as coming from the extensive bay of that name; while the N winds—which are by far the most dangerous—are named Gornaia Pogoda, or 'winds of the mountains,' because the shore from which they spring is particularly mountainous. In the neighbourhood of the B., the climate is extremely severe, owing chiefly to the elevation of the ground, and the want of sufficient shelter from the N winds. Its alt. above sea-level is 1,350 ft. [Erman] Scarcely a night, in the short summers which there diversify the year, passes without frost; and, even in August, the approach of winter is announced by frequent falls of snow. The lake, however, is never frozen over till late in December; nor so as to allow travellers to cross it before the 22d of January; and the ice generally dissolves about the beginning of May. Ice-fields, several miles in length, are first formed in the bays; and while congelation is going on, the rest of the lake is covered with a thick fog, till the whole becomes one solid mass, which, according to the calmness or agitation of the surface during the process of freezing, is either smooth as a mirror, or so rough as to be scarcely passable. The violent winds prevent the snow from adhering to it; so that to travel over it at first is an undertaking of extreme BAI difficulty. Sometimes the driver, while running by the side of his sledge, is thrown forward by a sudden squall, to the distance of several fathoms; and is in danger either of being frozen, or of falling into chinks left in the ice. As the time of breaking-up approaches, these chinks become wider and more frequent; boards are then laid across them for the accommodation of travellers; and when the apertures can no longer be passed in that manner, canoes begin to ply between the fields of ice. This dangerous mode of conveyance, however, is not long necessary; for, when the thaw once commences, the ice is very rapidly dissolved. In some of the bays which are shaded by the impending mountains from the sun, large masses of ice lie unmelted throughout the whole summer. It is seldom that its outlet the Angara freezes at all the first 20 versts from its source. [Cottrell.] A lake distinguished by such bold and singular features, fills all who behold it with astonishment and awe; and is regarded by the superstitious inhabitants of the surrounding country with a very natural veneration. They dignify it with the name of the Holy sea; to call it simply a lake, they would consider such a degree of profanity as cannot fail to provoke the immediate vengeance of heaven. Its name appears to be derived from the language of the Jakouts who formerly resided in its vicinity, and who called it Baya-khel or 'the Great sea,'-or Bai-khel, 'the Rich lake.' The Chinese call it Pe-hai, or 'the sea of the North;' the Buriats Dalai, and the Tounghouses Lam,—a name simply signifying 'a large collection of water,' or 'a sea.' The B. was first navigated by Europeans in 1643; but it was not until 1772 that a chart was published of it. In 1806 a new chart was published. The immediate vicinity of the B. is thinly peopled.—Pallas.—Gmelin.—Cottrel.—Erman.

BAIKHA, a river of Siberia, in the gov. of Tomsk. It takes its rise in the E of the district of Turukhansk, runs towards the W, and after a course of 150 m. falls into the Turukhan 45 m. W of the town of that name.—To the N of this river another named Vernaia-Baikha falls also into the Turukhau.
